Comment: Only quantity phrases with a noun in the genitive singular or essive do not agree with verbal predicates.
Habitual meaning is covered by a frequentative marker, see UT017.
Both frequentative and habitual meanings are expressed by the same suffix, see UT016.
Telic verbs in the present tense refer to the future. However, atelic verbs in the present tense can only refer to the future in the context of planned activities.
There is no evidence for main clauses with only infinitive predicates. However, their existence cannot be excluded, since they are possible in the better-documented neighbouring Saami languages.

Ellipsis of the subject is common in the corpus. However, no predicates that do not express a subject by default have been attested. Predicates, which typically do not show an overt subject, such as weather verbs, use a different strategy in Akkala Saami. Keeping in mind a slight degree of uncertainty, this parameter can be marked as 0 in Akkala Saami.

Such forms have not been found in Zaikov's grammar (1987) or text collection (1988). However, since the corpus is limited and such forms exist in very closely related languages, one cannot be completely sure of the absence of the non-finites in question. Keeping in mind a slight degree of uncertainty, this parameter can be marked as 0 in Akkala Saami.
The non-finite form on -i͔m functions as both participle and action nominalisation. In both positions, core arguments are encoded with ACC/GEN.

This question is not directly discussed in Zaikov's (1987) grammar. A survey of the available corpus (Kert and Zaikov 1988) allows to find at least some traces of agreement in the predicative position. Existing examples are not entirely convincing and a certain degree of uncertainty remains.

Person/number markers have been attested on adpositions in closely related languages. Their presence in Akkala Saami cannot be completely excluded. However, it is equally probable that they have completely disappeared long before the language was documented. Keeping in mind a slight degree of uncertainty, this parameter can be marked as 0 in Akkala Saami.
A survey of the existing corpus yields two words for 'uncle', although there is no direct evidence for the concrete meaning, the etymology suggests the semantic contrast in question.

There is no evidence for the semantic contrast in question in Akkala Saami. However, it is likely to have existed in Akkala Saami, since it is present in the better-documented neighbouring Saami languages. In the absence of evidence, we are forced to mark this feature with '?'.
There is an animacy distinction only for 'new/young', one adjective 'old' is used for animate and inanimate. However, there is a specialized adjective 'eldest'.

The length of consonants and vowels is interconnected:long vowels appear before short consonants, and short vowels before long consonants. However, beyond the phonological section of Zaikov's grammar, there are many deviations from this pattern. While Zaikov (1987) attempts to argue for a phonological basis for vowel quality opposition. However, there is a clear contrast between single and geminated consonants. The situation remains somewhat unclear.

The length of consonants and vowels is interconnected:long vowels appear before short consonants, and short vowels before long consonants. However, beyond the phonological section of Zaikov's grammar, there are many deviations from this pattern. While Zaikov (1987) attempts to argue for a phonological basis for vowel quality opposition, the situation remains somewhat unclear.
There is a contrast between short and long vowels /i/ vs /iː/ and /ɛ/ vs /ɛː/. At least the opposition /i/ vs /iː/ is independent of the quantity of consonants.
Zaikov (1987) does not claim it explicitly, however, qualitative opposition in vowels in some examples should correlate to the contrast in duration of geminate consonants.
If geminates before short vowels can be interpreted as long geminates, one can distinguish three consonant quantities. However, it is not possible to establish a minimum triple.
Trisyllabic dactyl feet are also possible.

There is no evidence for morphologically conditioned stress, as there are no accentuated texts. However, since some morphemes in neighbouring Saami languages require obligatory secondary stress, it can be assumed that this is also the case in Akkala Saami. In the absence of evidence, we are forced to mark this feature with '?'.
Zaikov (1987:41) lists some rising diphthongs, but this is the result of misinterpreting single vowels followed by glides.
Allophone of /g/ occurring in the word-final position after a vowel and in intervocalic position.
/f/ occurs in Russian borrowings only.
No arguments can be presented to define the direction of derivation (verb > noun or noun > verb) in Akkala Saami.

Suffixes are more likely to be used to mark the possessor of some types of inalienable nouns, e.g. kinship terms, than of other nouns. However, the distinction between alienable and inalienable nouns is not really grammaticalised in this way.
The present tense is marked cumulatively with number and person of S/A participant.
The past tense is marked cumulatively with the number and person of the S/A participant, but for some stem types a separate tense and person marker can be distinguished.
Derived and underived verbs have different markers for IMP.PL and for PRS.2PL
There is a polysemy of reflexive and reciproc.
Both SVO and SOV are pragmatically unmarked.
Both SOV and SVO are pragmatically unmarked.
There is a special converb to mark simultaneous action, but its use is not obligatory.
Constructions with floating qauntifier are common as well.

In neighbouring closely related languages there are such reciprocal markers. It is highly probable that a marker of this type also existed in Akkala Saami, but has not been attested due to the limited size of the corpus.
The existing corpus does not provide enough material on passive clauses.

In neighbouring closely related languages there is an inclusory construction. It is highly probable that this construction also existed in Akkala Saami, but has not been attested due to the limited size of the corpus.
Content interrogatives are possible in situ only in very rare cases.
Non-agreement is, however, fairly rare.
The frequentative suffix expresses continuation, which makes the situation unbounded.
The present form may be used to express future time reference; the form may also contain an inchoative suffix (-ška < -škanden).
Negation is formed using a negative particle.
The conditional mood is used with various time reference: sö-iži-n´, sönu-iži-n´, ol-izi-n´ sönu, olnu-iži-n´ sönu (< söda 'to eat').

Typically, the conditional mood is used.

It is common to use the copula, e.g. Mina ole-n irdou (1SG be-1SG outside) 'I'm outside'.

In Central Veps, goal and location marking differ, e.g. kažinno 'by the cat' vs. kažinnoks 'to/beside the cat'.
In Veps, the third set is used to express proximity. There is syncretism in GOAL and LOCATION marking.

In Central Veps the prolative marker -mu ~ -(d)me is used, e.g. mindai-me (1SG-PROL) 'with me'. The instrumental function is expressed with the adessive case, e.g. luziko-u (spoon-ADE) 'with a spoon'.
A postposition is used, e.g. minu-n täht (1SG-GEN for) 'for me'.

In East Mansi, the negation is symmetric, e.g., om lyoonk-ǝm kansy-iilǝm : om lyoonk-ǝm öät kansy-iilǝm (1SG way-PX.SG>1SG know-PRS.SG>1SG : 1SG way-PX.SG>1SG NEG know-PRS.SG>1SG) 'I know my way : I do not know my way'.
Personal passive is occasionally used, e.g., minǝxʷ ti min-wǝ ton kʷäl töärǝ (go-INF so go-PASS.3SG that house trough) 'they go continously through that house'.
The present participle is A/S oriented, whereas the past participle can refer to A, S, and P (teem ’who has eaten/which is eaten’).
"The caritive suffix attached to the verb stem produces a verbal noun, the use of which resembles a participle, and which modifies the noun".

Adpositions show syncretism, e.g. kʷäl ji-poål-t (house back-side-LOC) 'behind the house' is used to mark location and goal; cf. kʷäl ji-poål-nǝl (house back-side-ABL) 'from behind the house'.

All clauses except predicative clauses include a verb. However, while predicative 3rd person clauses never contain a copula, a copula may be occasionally used in other persons.

Indefinite objects are in the nominative case, whereas definite objects are typically in the genitive case.

Transitive verbs can agree with the object in person and number. Verbs in the objective conjugation express a bounded action, whereas verbs in the subjective conjugation (with definite objects) express unbounded actions. Furthermore, a definite object can also be in the inessive (the verb is in the subjective conjugation in this case) and this construction also expresses an unbounded action.
PST2 expresses past habituality.
In addition to expressing repeated events, PST2 can also express events that were continuing in the past.
The forms of present cover both present and future time.

Aspectual oppositions can be expressed with the objective vs. subjective conjugation. In the past tense, the negative marker takes person marking, and the two conjugation types are differentiated in the negative marker.
The infinitival participle -ma can mark necessity. The subject has to be in the dative case in this construction.
The passive/reflexive marker v is used to express capability, possibility, passive voice (depending on a context).

2PL and 3PL subject are syncretic in the objective conjugation.

Coordinating conjunctions are all of Russian origin (Hamari and Ajanki 2022:428). With nouns different types of coordinating is also possible, i.e., Pet'a-t-Mar'a-t (Pet'a-PL-Mar'a-PL) 'Pete and Mary' or Pet'a Mar'a marto (Pet'a Mar'a with) 'Pete with Mary' (Niina Aasmäe, p.c. 2020).
In existential sentences, the negation particle aras' is used. In other sentences, the negation particle a is used in the present tense and the negation verb ez' in the past tense.
In the present tense, negation is symmetric, e.g. kort-an : a kort-an (speak-PRS.1SG : NEG speak-PRS.1SG) 'I speak : I don't speak'.

The abessive can be used as a case-ending or in an adnominal function as an adjective. The latter function might be more frequent. Therefore, the abessive is not considered as a case-ending in most of the grammars.
The inessive -so/-se can also be used as instrumental, e.g., salmuks-so (needle-INS) 'by/with a needle'.
The comparative is used to mark the size or the approximate quantity of a referent.
The dative is used (see UT082).
Erzya has three declension types: basic declension, definite declension (expresses that the referent of the noun is known to the speaker and the listener) and the possessive declension.
However, there are also syncretic forms: singular and plural possession overlap in most cases.

Not discussed.

The pronoun kije 'who' is not used, kona 'who, that, which' is used instead. It appears with animates: loman'-es'/buka-s', kona sa-s' t'eze (person-DEF.NOM/ox-DEF.NOM that come-PST1.3SG here) 'The person/ox that came here' as well as with inanimates t'el'e-s', kona juta-s' (winter-DEF.NOM which pass-PST1.3SG) 'the winter that is over'.
See the comment in UT107.
The possessive suffixes usually follow case suffixes. In some cases, however, case suffixes and possessive suffixes are fused.
The singular is unmarked, whereas in the plural the plural suffix t : t' follows the noun, and it is followed by the definiteness marker and the case ending.
леля; l'el'a 'uncle' is used for both.
There is only distinction for 'old', i.e. tašto 'old (inanimate), sir'e 'old (animate)'.

There is only distinction for 'cold': jakšamo 'cold' is used about (winter) weather, day (cf. also jakšamo-s' (cold-DEF) 'frost, cold weather), whereas kel'me 'cold' is used in a wide range of meanings, e.g. moda-s' kel'me (soil-DEF cold) 'the soil is cold', ked'-et' kel'me (hand-POSS.2SG cold) 'your hand is cold'.

Geminate consonants only occur on morpheme boundary. They may arise when a consonant-initial suffix is attached to a consonant stem, e.g., мaкссa mɑks-sɑ (give-PRS.3SG>3SG) (Hamari and Ajanki 2022:397). As regards consonant duration, there are phonetically short consonants in the language, and there are geminates, but the potential opposition is not part of a quantity system, since this is a morhophonologically conditioned difference (Lehiste et al. 2003:85).

The inherent length of vowels in a word, rather than stress affects the relationship between vowel lengths - stressed U may be shorter than unstressed a: nUla 'a piece of cloth'; intrinsically, the high vowels, u, i are shorter than the low a; the length of the stressed i, u may be equal to (or even longer than) that of the unstressed a.
No genuine geminates; at morpheme boundary, false geminates may be formed: van-n-oms (see-FRQ-INF).
Quantity has no phonological significance.

Mainly disyllabic trochaic feet are realized in speech, e.g. kUdo-sO-nzo (home-INE-POSS.3SG) 'in his/her home'. Trisyllabic feet, e.g. kUdo-so-st (home-INE-POSS.3PL) are also possible. Stress can move across word boundaries: c'Ora-t'-n'E ez'-t' tUje-vE kudO-v (man-PL-NOM.DEF NEG-PST1.3PL leave-PASS.CNG home-LAT) 'The men did/could not leave for home'. Stress is not distinctly expressed.
Word stress is relatively free and it does not distinguish between meanings.
The variants of the emphatic particle (clitic) -jak/-kak/-gak 'even, too' (e.g. sOn-gAk 'he, too') tend to have primary or secondary stress.

The first syllable tends to be stressed if the word is uttered as a dictionary item; in words with more than two syllables, stressed and unstressed syllables alternate: mOdamAr' 'potato' (Niina Aasmäe, p.c. 2020). Stress does not consitute identificational stress but utterance rhythmic structure (Lehiste et al. 2003:86).
Initial and non-initial stress alternate: mon E-z'in' mO-l'e /mOn e-z'In' mo-l'E (1SG.NOM NEG.PST-1SG go.CNG) 'I did not go'.
There are only monophtongs.
There are only monophtongs.

In South-Eastern dialects, reduced vowels occur in unstressed syllables (showing different patterns of non-contrustive distribution); in some words, a vowel having the quality of a reduced V may appear in a stressed syllable: pUromo-ms/pUrǝmǝ-ms/pƏrǝmǝ-ms 'get together' (Niina Aasmäe, p.c. 2020).
In South-Eastern dialects, /e/ vs. /æ/: keď 'hand, arm' vs. käď 'leather, skin'; word-final e, i > ä: śimi > śimä 's/he drinks' .
They occur mainly in suffixes (as the result of elision).
CC(C) occurring mainly in suffixes (as the result of vowel elision).
Before front vowels, labials and velars may be palatalized.
Dialect variation: kov/koŋ 'moon, month', positional nasalization n + k, g: kengeľ-ems 'to tell lies', ponkst 'trousers', kenkš 'a door' (Niina Aasmäe, p.c. 2020).
raužo 'black', raśke 'ethnos, people', ruma-ms 'become bleached', Rav 'Volga', ruz 'Russian'.

In the objective conjugation, if P is in 1st or 2nd person and either A, P or both are plural, the suffixes are ambiguous as to the number of A and P and the person of A. Other objective suffixes have different patterns of ambiguity and some are entirely unambiguous.
However, possible with 'feed'.
The verb karmams 'to begin' can be used as a future auxiliary.

Productively expressed by the -vo-, -ve- suffix attached to the root. Another passive suffix, -to- can be found in folkloric and spoken texts.

For example, reduplication is used to form distributive adverbs, e.g., ʃkɑnj-ʃkɑnj ‘at times, occasionally’. There are also reduplicated pronouns.
The adjective paro 'good' can agree with the head noun in number. In certain varieties also other adjectives can allow number agreement.
Adnominal demonstratives agree with their head noun in number, but not in case.
The definite declension features different endings for singular and plural, within these endings different morphemes can be identified as definiteness markers.
Several productive suffixes: -ńe, -ińe, -ke.
Adjectives most commonly precede their head nouns.
vese 'all' precedes its head noun; it can also be used standalone in the meaning of 'all', 'everyone'.
Eŕva 'every' vs. vese 'all'.

However, the ablative case used to mark the standard in comparative constructions has limited use in the expression of spatial properties.
The postposition коряс ‘with respect to’ can be used to mark the standard.
The pronoun śeďe, originally an ablative form of singular demonstrative pronoun, can be used, especially if the standard is not mentioned.

The word pŕa 'head' (also with possessive suffixes) can be used as a reflexive object marker. For other uses, both in reflexive and emphatic meanings, declined forms of the pronoun eś are used, also suffixes to personal pronouns.
Applies to finite clauses formed with relative pronouns.
Applies to non-finite clauses formed with participles.

Pronouns and definite nouns are marked with the genitive case, sometimes called genitive-accusative. However, some verbs can additionally or exclusively govern other cases, especially ablative, which if optional denotes a partial quantity (used as partitive).
However, indefinite objects of verbs appear in nominative.
Polite usage of 2PL forms in place of the expected 2SG may be stigmatised by some speakers as purported Russian influence.
When the possessor is explicitly mentioned, this suffix is only added if certain conditions regarding specificity of said possessor are fulfilled.
Optative mood is rare and, with some exceptions, generally not used in objective conjugation. Instead, an analytic construction with the particle kadik 'let' is preferred.
The use of the partitive case is common with imperfcetive/unbounded situations, whereas the genitive occurs with perfective/bounded situations.
The nominative marking of the object is common in imperative clauses. In negative clauses, the partitive object is typically used.

/ʃ/ (written with letters š and ž) occurs only in foreign words, and there are some minimal pairs with contrast of /ʃ/ and /s/ (e.g., šokk 'shock' – sokk 'sock; goat', šikk 'chic' – sikk 'goat', loož 'loge' – loos 'story, sg.iness').

/f/ occurs only in foreign words, and there are some minimal pairs with contrast of /f/ and /v/ (e.g., farss 'farce' – varss 'foal', faas 'phase' – vaas 'vase'); consonant cluster /hv/ can have a variant [ff] as in kohvik [koffikː] 'café', rahvas [rɑffɑs] 'people', kahvel [kɑfːfel] 'fork'.
There are indefinite pronouns such as üks, mingi.
IMP.1PL has inclusive meaning (Erelt and Metslang 2017:197).

The object may appear in the nominative (unmarked), genitive, or partitive case.
The object may appear in the nominative (unmarked), genitive, partitive, or accusative case.

However, it is more common to express predicative possession by means of a locative coded possessor.

Instances of agreement in case can not be attested.
One of the options is to express the subject of non-finite clause by an overt NP in oblique.
P is marked in the same way in finite and non-finite clauses.

However, only very few nouns distinguish between nominative and oblique in non-possessed forms, where properties of the object can be a decisive factor. For marking in the verb, topicality of the object is the most important factor.

In imperative clauses, the object is usually encoded with the nominative. However, also accusative marking can be found, which could be Russian influence as such marking cannot be attested in the speech of previous generations.
"Irrespective of whether a clause is active or passive, its subject can be omitted if its referent is identical with that of the subject referent of the preceding clause."
Habitual is used to express a regular event.
This needs further research.
The aorist has no overt morpheme; the basic form of aorist refers to the past with perfective verbs.

The subjunctive "expresses a hypothetical event with the basic tense-modal series, and an irrealis event with the past TM-series". Subjunctive is common in Forest Enets, very rare in modern Tundra Enets.
An analytic construction consisting of a conditional converb of the auxiliary with anterior participle is used.
Debitive also expresses reference to the future.
The probabilitive and interrogative can be used in this function.
The soft imperative is used to express mild commands.
It is not reflexive in meaning, but traditionally called so.
Plural or dual object in 2DU and 3DU are both marked by -ziʔ.
With the verbs of motion, the converb or supine is used, while with other verbs the converb is used.
There are verbs which can never have an overt argument, and those which can be used with and without an overt argument.
Locative clauses are formed with copulas ɛ-/ŋa-.
dʲagu- 'be absent(IPFV)'
Clausal negation involves a negative verb + a lexical verb in the non-finite connegative form.
There is a negative anterior participle (see also UT058).
Passive anterior participle (-duuj) relativizes objects, for all other participles there are frequency tendencies, not strict specializations.
All participles are unoriented, except passive anterior participle. (The perfective participle has two allomorphs: -iʔ and -miʔ).
Relativization of participants other than subject and object is possible but very rare.
The respective participle is called negative anterior participle.
The posterior participle can be used to express future.
Both OV and VO are present; OV could be more frequent, but a separate study is needed.

Only an adverb, a personal pronoun in the accusative form, or a non-finite complement verb may stand between negative and lexical verbs, though this happens very rarely. All examples with accusative personal pronoun and a negative imperative verb show the O NEG V-CNG order in the corpus.
In the predicative position, the stative intransitive verb behaves like a verb.
However, there are postpositions that have never been attested with posessive suffixes.
Pronouns have three core cases (NOM, GEN, ACC), while nouns have only two (NOM and OBL).

However, the caritive does not combine with possessive suffixes and personal pronouns, and it does not have number forms.
Dative marks goal and other meanings, e.g., recipient, beneficiary, etc.
Locative also has other usages.
Ablative also has other usages.
Locative is also used in instrumental and comitative functions.
Locative is also used in instrumental and comitative functions.
The translative is a recent grammaticalization. It is not formed from the stem but from the nominative. The translative does not take possessive suffixes and it is not attested with peronal pronouns.
There is no separate case. The case is referred to as the essive-translative case.
There is only a similative suffix.

3SG possessive suffixes are used less commonly than 2SG possessive suffixes.
The pronoun ʃee 'who' is also used for bear, but it is an exception. For all other non-human referents ɔbu 'what ' is used.

Relative clauses usually occur before the head noun in the NP.
The order becomes apparent from locative forms, and the order is fixed. Core cases (i.e. NOM and OBL) are always fused with possessive forms.

Enets has double or long vowels. However, cf. Helimski (1984), who analyses them as a sequence of two identical vowel phonemes.

Personal pronouns inflect for the nominative, genitive, and accusative. However, genitive rarely occurs.
Each verb is lexically perfective or imperfective, there is no morphological distinction between them.
Direct objects appear in the nominative or oblique case, while recipients are typically coded with the dative or predestinative case.
There are various causative suffixes (marginal applicative uses).
There is a reflexive pronoun.
There is a reciprocal pronoun.
Pragmatically neutral word order in transitive clauses is SOV (see GB133). Finite complement clauses follow the structure SVmatrix COMPL (=OBJ).
Adnominal property words show no inflection. However, since the plural marker -ʔ is often realized as zero, it cannot be ruled out that occasionally they can agree in number.
Modifiers usually precede the head noun.
all' is a floating quantifier.
There is no special morphology.
Possible with past time reference.
Alternative questions can be formed using repetition of the clause.
The dative is used to mark an agent in a passive construction.
The reflexive pronoun originally means 'body'.
The reciprocal pronoun originally means 'mate, relative'.
There are three suppletive stems but if a pronoun is expressed then it is typically the nominative form.
The so called auditive is used to express an event that was either audially inferred by the speaker or that was reported to the speaker.
There is a modal-interrogative verb ɔbujta- ‘what for’.
They can occur both fronted and in situ.
In the case of non-possessed forms it is possible that nouns do not distinguish between core cases.
Not mentioned.
Both the nominative and oblique case are possible, however, the latter is less common.
reported discourse marker man'

There is no case agreement within noun phrases at all.

“Possessive suffixes can be added to the long form of the gerund of prior action. /.../ Hill Mari generally uses nominative forms of personal pronouns in combination with gerunds marked for possessive suffixes rather than genitive forms (Krasnova et al. 2017:170).
Differential object marking is only possible in non-finite clauses.

There is differential object marking only in non-finite clauses. e.g. кол кыч-аш [kol kət͡ʃ-ɑʃ] (fish.NOM catch-INF) ‘to fish, to catch fish’, кол-ым кыч-аш [kol-əm kət͡ʃ-ɑʃ] (fish-ACC catch-INF) ‘to catch a fish’. The motivation for using nominative objects presumably lies on the intersection of definiteness and aspectuality.

Compound past tenses I & II (formed by present-tense form followed by ‘to be’ in the third person singular, either in simple past I or II) are habitual (the distinction between them is evidential). Also, aspectual auxiliaries (light verbs) илаш ‘to live’ and шогалаш ‘to stand up’ can be coupled with a converb and give the resulting pairing a habitual value.

Numerous defivational suffixes: -л [-l]: лӹмдӓш [lə̈mdɑʃ] ‘to name’ → лӹмдӹлӓш [lə̈mdə̈læʃ] ‘to call names’, -ешт [-eʃt]: колаш [kolɑʃ] ‘to hear’ → колышташ [koləʃtɑʃ] ‘to listen’, -кал [-kɑl]: айыраш [ɑjərɑʃ] ‘to separate’ → айыркалаш [ɑjərkɑlɑʃ] ‘to sort’, -ед [-ed]: пуаш [puɑʃ] ‘to give’ → пуэдӓш [puedæʃ] ‘to hand out’. Also auxiliary опташ ‘to place (several items)’.
Aspectual auxiliary (light verb) ӹлӓш [ə̈læʃ] ‘to live’ is used.
Especially in combination with atelic verbs or verbal pairings, the verb тӹнгӓлӓш [tə̈ŋgælæʃ] ‘to begin’ is used as a future auxiliary.
Simple past tense 1 forms can, in a structure presumably borrowed from Russian, be used to denote something that is about to happen in the immediate future.

The usual way of expressing this is using the converb of prior action. Also simple past III & IV (distinction between them is evidential), formed by a simple past II form followed by a past-tense form (simple past I and II respectively) of the verb ‘to be’ in the third person singular, are “used to denote an action that preceded another action or is interrupted by it”.

The particle ылын [ələn] (‘to be’ in simple past II, 3SG) is placed after verbs in sentences “in reference to inferences, knowledge that was previously unknown, etc.” - including surprising events. However, there is no grammatical disctinction between evidential and mirative forms: тӹдӹ шыжар-жы доно ӓрня нӓрӹ хынален кашт-еш ыл-ын [tə̈də̈ ʃəʒɑr-ʒə dono ærɲæ nærə̈ xənɑlen kɑʃt-eʃ əl-ən] (3SG little_sister-PX3SG with week about for_a_visit go-PRS.3SG) 'It turned out that s/he stayed at her/his sister’s for about a week'.

A conditional suffix -гецӹ [-get͡sə̈] can be found, but is marginal in usage: in modern language it is only used on the verb ‘to be’, ылгецӹ [əlget͡sə̈]; this form is used as a particle in combination with indicative forms.
There is a necessitive infinitive with the ending -мыла/-мӹлӓ [-məlɑ/-mə̈læ] and a future-necessitive participle in -шашлык/-шашлӹк [-ʃɑʃlək/-ʃæʃlək].
The future-necessitive participle in --шашлык/-шӓшлык [-ʃɑʃlək/-ʃæʃlək], which prototypically denotes necessity, can in some cases be used in this function. See also UT028.
Realized by a highly productive derivational suffix.
Probably Russian influence.
This typically happens in Mari (only) with causative verb forms. The verbs meaning ‘rain’, ‘snow’ , and ‘blow’ (wind) require a subject.
Copula is not needed 3rd person form of the present tense, e.g. тӹдӹ тымдышы [tə̈də̈ təmdəʃə] (3SG teacher) ‘s/he is a teacher’.
The element -ды-/-дӹ -[-də- ~ -tə- ~ -də̈- ~ -tə̈-] contained in numerous non-finite forms.
Realized by a highly productive derivational suffix.
While the participle in -дымы is historically complex (negation element + ending of passive participle), it is treated as a unit in modern language.
No agreement between modifiers and superordinate nouns in general; participles do not differ from this norm.
Possible with all participles but the active participle.
The basic word order is SOV. There is no obvious deviation from the norm in the imperative, at least not on a regular basis

The negation verb and the connegative form are tied to one another tightly. It is unusual for other elements (e.g., adverbs, pronouns) to occupy the space between them and it is unusual for the word order to change – the connegative form by default follows the negation verb.
The accusative encodes the object in finite sentences.
There is no agreement, e.g. нӹнӹ пуры ыл-ыт [nə̈nə̈ purə əl-ət] (3PL kind be-PRS.3PL) ‘they are kind’.
Analytic formation is used: сек яжо (SPRL good) ‘the best’;сек < Russian всех.
The nominative case is used, e.g. карандаш [kɑrɑndɑʃ] ‘pencil’, кок карандаш [kok kɑrɑndɑʃ] ‘two pencils’.
One of the prototypical usage situations for the dative, which unlike the dative in Turkic or Ob-Ugric languages is not used in a spatial sense.
The so-called comitative case mentioned in Mari grammars and textbooks has a radically different function.
“The comparative case is used in answer to the questions кӱлӓ [kylæ]? ‘like whom, as whom’ and мала? ‘like what, as what’”
The dative is used to mark benefactors.

It should be noted that inanimate nouns traditionally did not occur in the genitive at all; inanimate nouns serving as a modification do occur in the nominative, e.g. Вена университет [Venɑ universitet] (Vienna.NOM university) ‘University of Vienna’ (see mrj.wikipedia.org/wiki/%D0%92%D0%B5%D0%BD%D0%B0)

The possessive suffix is used to indicate a reference to something previously discussed or otherwise salient to the conversation. It is especially commonly used to refer to a subset of a larger whole that is salient.

“Both in Hill Mari and in Meadow Mari, the pronouns тидӹ [tidə̈] (тиде [tide]) ‘this’ and тӹдӹ [tə̈də̈] (тудо [tudo]) ‘that’ have short forms ти [ti] ~ тӹ [tə̈] (ты [tə] ~ ту [tu]). The long forms are used when they stand on their own and are subject to nominal inflection. Before a noun as an attribute, in Hill Mari only the short form is used, whereas in Meadow Mari either the short or long form can be used.”
The “degrees of freedom” depend on the case.

The same word is used in both occasions, e.g. шокшы игечӹ [ʃokʃo iget͡ʃə̈] ‘hot weather’ - шокшы вӹд [ʃokʃo və̈d] ‘hot water’; ӱштӹ игечӹ [yʃtə̈ iget͡ʃə̈] ‘cold weather’ - ӱштӹ вӹд [yʃtə̈ və̈d] ‘cold water’.
Geminate (long) consonants only occur at morpheme boundaries. нӹллӹ [nə̈lːə̈] ‘forty’ (← нӹл [nə̈l] ‘four’ + лу [lu] ‘ten’)
Reduced vowels are shorter than full vowels, but there is a clear qualitative difference between non-reduced and reduced vowels, so it cannot be reasoned that vowel length alone is distinctive.
No distinctive vowel length at all.
No distinctive vowel length at all; geminate consonants only at morpheme boundaries.
In Hill Mari, some suffixes are stressed (usually the penultimate syllable is stressed in Hill Mari), e.g. the comparative suffix.

The penultimate syllable is generally stressed: ӓтямлӓн [æˈcæmlæn] ‘to my father’. However: ( 1 ) a number of suffixes are stressed in violation of these rules, ( 2 ) if the penultimate syllable has a reduced vowel and there is a full vowel preceding that syllable, the stress shifts to the previous syllable.
Syllable structure has no transparent effect on where stress falls.

The penultimate syllable is generally stressed. However: ( 1 ) a number of suffixes are stressed in violation of these rules, ( 2 ) if the penultimate syllable has a reduced vowel and there is a full vowel preceding that syllable, the stress shifts to the previous syllable.
Consistent palato-velar vowel harmony.
In Hill Mari, consistent palato-velar vowel harmony.
There are however two reduced vowels with similar values: ы [ə], ӹ [ə̈].
Very rare and the effect of a fairly recent loss of reduced vowels that did not happen in all varieties of Mari.
The palatal consonants [ʎ], [ɲ], [c] are frequently mislabelled as palatalized. Palatalized pronunciation under Russian influence and especially in Russian loan words does happen.
Only in Russian loan words among contemporary speakers.
However, phonemic status is debated.
Historically as allophones of the fricatives /β/, /ɣ/, /ð/ after homorganic nasals, i.e.: [mb], [ŋg], [nd]. Today /ɣ/ and /ð/ are widely pronounced as [g] and [d].

It is uncommon, but not excluded. There are some words of proto-Uralic origin that have an initial r. There are some varieties in which intial r is preceded by prothetic ə, however, presumably under Chuvash indluence.
Today historic /ɣ/ is commonly pronounced as [g].
Cf. Meadow Mari калык [kɑlək].
Today historic [ð] is commonly pronounced as [d].
Among younger speakers it is commony pronounced as [v].
In Hill Mari, “/f/ is often said to have phonemic value in Hill Mari, but its usage is quite restricted here as well”.
The comitative postposition don(o), however, can be used to express nominal conjunction.
Characteristic for preterite only.
There are two conjugation classes.
There are three causative markers: -t (with the allomorph -d), -ə̑kt (with the allomorph ‐ӛkt), and ‐tar (with the allomorphs ‐tär, ‐dar, ‐där, ‐ar, ‐är). The most frequent is -ə̑kt.
The mediopassive suffix -alt- can express reflexive meaning, but only with a limited set of verbs (see also GB115).
The mediopassive suffix -alt- can express reciprocal meaning (see also GB114). .
Analytic past tenses (see GB121) may be used to express habitual and durative actions in the past.
The syntactic parameters of main and subordinate finite clauses are the same, i.e. the consituent order therein may vary (see GB136).

Non-finite clausal objects typically occupy the position before the verb, similar to nominal objects. The position of finite clauses is conditioned by the type of complementizer and the complement type (Kaškin et al. 2023:734).
There is variation in the ordering of core argument constituents, conditioned by information-structural parameters.
There is a passive participle with the suffix ‐mə̑ /‐mӛ. The mediopassive suffix -alt- has the proper passive meaning.
Only when the demonstrative moves out of the NP, such an agreement is possible (Kaškin et al. 2023:203).
сilä 'all, whole', každə̑j 'each'
In some dialects, the position of the interrogative particle can be predetermined by the information structure.
The Russian comparative adposition čem 'than' is used in comparative constructions.
However, the debitive and optative moods are marked by the negation particle agə̑l.
The analytic past tense, with the main verb in the preterite, creates a sense of distance from the Time of Reference.
E.g. lu 'ten', šə̈m lu 'seventy'.
The two complementizers što and štobə̑, borrowed from Russian, appear in preposed position.
See GB120.
See GB121.
In some cases, definite objects with possessive suffixes are not in accusative (Kittilä et al. 2022:883).
Intonational pattern may indicate surprise (Kenesei et al. 1998:432).
Epistemic possibility can be encoded on the verb with the derivational suffix -hat/-het.
The suffix -hat/-het may also encode permission.
Usually it is necessary to specify what is raining but when it becomes clear from the context it is enough to say esik 'raining'.
There are locative constructions without copula: Előtte az élet ‘Life is before him/her.’ (Kenesei et al. 1998: 186).
To compare, standard negation is expressed using "nem": Nem megyek haza (NEG go-PRS.1SG home) 'I do not go home'.
Active present participle relativizes subjects.
The future participle also expresses necessity.
Participles do not encode the subject, e.g. Az általam megcsinált munka / A megcsinált munkám.

Diminutive marking is possible, but it is used only by some people when talking to children, e.g. Mosikázd meg a kezecskéidet evés előtt (wash-DIM-IMP.2SG.OC PRV DEF hand-DIM-PL-POSS.2SG eating before) 'Wash your hands before eating'.
In dialectal and nonstandard speech case-marking is sometimes doubled.
However, it is more common to use the postposition nélkül 'without'.

There is syncretism in the spoken Hungarian: both the illativ and inessiv case may sound -ba/-be, e.g. könyvtárba megyek 'I am going to the library', olvasok a könyvtárba(n) 'I am reading in the library', where -n does not sound.
There is a distinction between internal and external local cases, and there is also a third set used to express proximity.
The plurality of the possessed noun is expressed by the suffix ˗i inserted between the possession-marker and the person-marker.
ki 'who' is sometimes used for animals but such usage does not follow the standards. However, such usage is possible in dialects.
Usage of a demonstrative pronoun for human referent tends to have a pejorative meaning.
It seems that secondary stressed derivational suffixes often contain a long vowel.

The orthographic ly, which originally represented a palatal(ized) l, is in Modern Standard Hungarian merely another spelling for /j/ in certain words. /ʎ/ can only be found in the Central-Palóc subdialects and Eastern subdialects.
Only as an allophone of /h/.
It occurs only as a constituent of the falling diphthong [ɔw], as in autó ‘automobile’.
However, possible with ‘feed’ and 'teach'.
For examples, for a large class of verbs, 3Sg is marked with the suffix -ik.
However, the derivation of causatives from noncausatives is not a completely productive process in Hungarian.
Topic constituent must be referential and specific.
Reduplication of verbal prefixes can be attested; this adds a frequentative meaning.
The question clitic -e can be added to the verb.
Participial clauses always precede the head noun.
The finite verbs agree with the nominal subject. Non-agreement is possible only between plural and dual in certain sentence types, like habitual and existential.

Adjectives in Inari Saami don't usually agree with the nouns, instead an attributive form is used with adjectives, e.g. puáris tááluin (old.ATTR house.PL.LOC) 'in old houses'. Pronouns and numerals agree in some cases fully and in some cases partially, e.g. tain tááluin (this.PL.LOC house.PL.LOC) 'in these houses', taan táálust (this.SG.GEN house.SG.LOC) 'in this house'. Finally, there are some adjectives that either are considered to follow the same rule as the pronouns and numerals, or as a variation have the attributive form.

The partial agreement cases in singular are the locative, illative, and abessive, in plural there is no agreement in comitative and abessive. With these cases the adnominal property word is in the genitive form (in SG or PL).
It is possible to derive frequentative verbs from other verbs with various derivative markers.
There is no future tense in Inari Saami. The present tense can be used to mean something that will happen in the future.
Depending on the verb aspect, the immediate future reading is possible.
There is potential.

The IMP.1SG form is included in the Inari Saami dictionary (Sammallahti & Morottaja 1993) but there are no examples of its usage. It can be speculated to be used like this: Čáállum reeivâ (write-IMP.1SG letter-ACC) 'I should write a letter'.
IMP.2PL and INF are syncretic.

There is also a caritive derivation for nouns and also for verbs, e.g., viste > viste-ttem 'house - houseless', jäämmiđ > jämi-mettum 'to die - never dying'. There is also an abessive case, meaning 'without': viste : viste-ttáá 'house' : 'without house' (see also UT084).
luuhâm ja luhhum are the same form of a participle but from a different verb (luuhâđ 'to read' and luhhuđ 'to be read').
The negative participle is a counterpart for participles and converbs.
Agentive passive participle and action nominalization are identical.
Present participle can be used to express an agent.
There are diminutive (perhaps not hypocoristic) verbal derivates, e.g. smakkiđ > smakkii-stiđ 'to taste - to taste a little'.
There is the same case for source and location (locative case).

If NP is in Nominative or Accusative case, the noun can have three different cases, 1 - Nominative, 2-6 - Genitive (accusative) or 7- ... - Partitive. If NP is in any other case, the noun can be inflected to any case and the number will partially agree with the noun.
The locative case marks both location and source.
The locative case marks both location and source.
The locative case marks for location and source.
Comitative case is used in the instrumental and comitative function.
There is the essive case, which has both essive and translative function.
tot 'it' can be used instead of sun 's/he'.
Usually the order is case+px . There is an exception for abessive, where the order is px+case. In comitative and abessive plural, the possessive suffix has divided the case suffix.
In the first example, the length of the consonant also co-varies, the second example contains a pure minimum pair.
Long non-initial vowels are secondary stressed in Inari Saami.
There are half-long and long geminates (see Türk 2019).
There is a three-way opposition: short, half-long, long (see Markus et al. 2012, Türk 2019).
For example, 1st syllable of the derivate -škyettiđ is always stressed independent of what kind of verb it is attached to.
See also UT122.
Lowering diphtongs are uo, ye, ie, uá, iä.
ü [y] may be part of the diphthong ye (as in kye'le), besides that it just appears in some words, e.g. pygálys, pövkkyr.
ö [ø] is present only in two loanwords: pövkkyr (< Fin. pöykkyri) and ministeriö (< Fin. ministeriö).
However, it can also be pronounced as [enːnuv].
When long, they are voiced, e.g. kobbâ 'white reindeer', säggi 'splinter', koddeđ 'to kill'.
[v] and [w] are in complementary distribution.
In general, subjects are marked in the plural even when the verb is in the dual. However, the dual suffix -kyevtis may carry additive or associative meaning (see Müller 2018).
Aanaar Saami lacks a dedicated associative plural marker, but the dual suffix -kyevtis can express both additive and associative meanings (see Müller 2018).
There are conjugation classes, but they are based on morphophonological alternations.
The causative suffix -tt- can transitivize intransitive sentences and add an additional role to an already transitive clause (Valtonen and Ylikoski and Aikio 2022: 191).
The verb kavnuđ ‘to be found’ has an existential reading, but semantically it is not as neutral as the copula leđe ‘to be’.
The reciprocal pronoun is kyeimi kuámán~kuáimád~kyeimis, the latter being marked with a possessive suffix. The word kyeimi also translates as ‘partner’ or ’friend’.
Non-finite relative clauses are prenominal.
However, in many cases, subject is realized by a zero pronoun.

Non-agreement appears in the 3rd person; in constructio ad sensum: NP is in singular after numeral 2, verb appears in plural. Also the use with subject in 3rd plural and verb in 3rd singular is attested.
There is no indication of object number in the objective conjugation in Kamas.
Occurs only in the case of inversion: Adj. N > N Adj.
There is no agreement in case or number in the NP.

A genitive subject with a participle + LOC + possessive suffix is expected. Unfortunately, in all attested instances (possessive suffixes, personal pronouns of the 1st/2nd persons) the genitive form is identical to the nominative.
Object marking depends on the givenness.
Zero marking occurs with the imperative, as in the other Samoyed languages.
No special marker for habituality is attested.
The markers for frequentative are =l and =Vr.
The most typical future tense marker is -LA. This does not, however, necessarily express immediate future.
The converb -bīzA is used.
There is no aspect derivation of the negation verb.
It is marked in the future tense (as opposed to the imperative and conditional). Present and past tenses operate with the negation particle.
Converb construction with auxiliary nu- ‘stand’ (not entirely sure, however).
There are two possibilities: 1) verbs derived with the suffix -nzə; 2) construction with the participle in -NTə + auxiliary mo- ‘become’.

Kamas has conditional mood. The marker of the conditional is -NTA...-ze; the first component is the participle in -NTA (-na ~ -ne after vowels, and -ta ~ -te ~ -da ~ -de after consonants), the second component is an enclitic morphem -ze which follows the personal ending.
Not attested.
3rd person imperative (jussive) is used.
1st person imperative (hortative) is used.
Not attested.
Not attested.
The variation between clauses with copula (ige ‘is’) and without is not understood; zero-copula predication, however, seems to be the default case (Klumpp 2016:66).
The conjunction ‘da’ is used.
The separate existential negation operator is the word ‘naga’.
There are two standard negation constructions in complementary distribution: 1) particle negation in PRS, PST, and with non-finite clauses; 2) negation verb in FUT, COND, and IMP.
Particle negation is used.
There is the caritive converb -žət (‘without x-ing’), elsewhere the standard negation particle is used.

There are the following options: 1) Interaction of valency derivation and participle, e.g. amor-o-na ‘eaten’ (P) vs. amor-tə-na ‘having eaten’ (S/A); 2) Participle in -gA: only S/A; 3) Participle in -mA: only P/S.
Participle in -NTA is used. See also UT055.
In nominalization, participles need a dummy noun of general meaning, e.g., kuza ‘man’ (Klumpp 2016:54).
SOV is the basic word order. The reverse order, VO, can occur in imperative clauses, but also in verum focus context. There are also examples of imperative at the end of a clause.
The accusative case is used.
Attested only in late Kamas.
Primary postpositions come without inflection; secondary postpositions, i.e. grammaticalized nouns with local semantics, are inflected for case and, possibly, also for person.
Secondary postpositions, i.e. grammaticalized nouns with local semantics, are inflected for case and, possibly, also for person.
Caritive -žət is rather a derivation, not a case.
Appears in possessive declension (lative-locative case).
There is an instrumental case with the ending -Śǝʔ.
The distinction is between animate vs. inanimate.
No relative pronoun use is attested.
No relative pronoun use is attested.
There is no difference between the demonstrative pronoun and the 3rd person singular referent, the same word is used for both.

The suffix order in the possessive declension is number suffix-case suffix-possessive suffix in all cases, except for the youngest case, the instrumental, which shows the opposite order (number suffix-possessive suffix-case suffix).
The suffix order is always number suffix before case suffix.
The words dʼăktə ‘old (of persons)’, kuju ‘old (of things)’, ďēt ‘young’ and tăbdə ‘new’ are attested.
Vowel length appears secondarily, from contraction.

Contraction of converb and aspectual auxiliary verb attratcs stress, e.g. kutla·ʔbi ‘caught’ (< kut-laʔ baʔ-bi ‘catching threw’); so while the converb -laʔ is unstressed, the aspectual morpheme -laʔ as in the example word is stressed.
If the first syllable is light (open, short or reduced vowel), and a following syllable heavy (closed, long vowel), accent moves to the heavy syllable.

The (probably inherited) pattern of assigning prominency to the first syllable of a word by expiratory accent, e.g. ka·m.bi ‘went’, is challenged by two divergent patterns: 1) if the first syllable is light (open, short or reduced vowel), and a following syllable heavy (closed, long vowel) accent moves to the heavy syllable, e.g. su.ra·r.ga ‘asking’, a.mo·r.zit.tǝ ‘to eat’; 2) the Turkic pattern of assigning accent to the last syllable has been noticed by Castrén and Donner, not only in Turkish loans, but also in genuine words.
Vowel harmonic patterning of the reduced vowel includes also labiality.

Kamas had eight full and two reduced vowels. The closed front vowel phonemes are /i/ and /ü/ (the latter is phonetically a rather central [ɯ]), the open front vowel phonemes are /e/ and /ö/ (the latter is phonetically often a delabialized [e̮]), and the reduced front vowel is /ĭ/, or /ə̈/ (the spelling as an [over]short ĭ is preferred here); an additional full front vowel phoneme would be /ɛ/, or /ä/. The back vowels mirror the front vowel system in terms of opening grades, labiality, and reducedness.
Appears in loanwords from Turkic and Russian.
Notorious problem; if yes, it’s marginal.
There is a free variation with the affricate ťś as an allophone. Appears also in Russian loanwords.
There is a convergence of palatal and palatalized nasal.
The postalveolar affricate č appears only in Turkic and Russian loanwords.
The palatal stop /ť/ has voiced allophones [ť] ~ [ď] which basically occur in free variation.
r does not occur word-initally, however, it occurs in syllable onset word internally.
Appears as an allophone of [g].
Appears in free variation with [k].
Appears in free variation with [d].
Appears in free variation with [b].
There are no definite articles. Differential object marking and possessive marked nouns can express definiteness.
There are no indefinite articles. Indefinitess is marked only within indefinite pronouns with a derivational suffix =də.
There are no articles.
There are no articles.
There is no grammatical gender in Kamas.
There are in total four demonstratives: neutral dĭ ‘this’, proximal dü ‘this here’, and the distal pronouns šö ‘that’ and idǝ ‘that’ (the difference between the latter two is not clear).
There is no information or examples indicating whether Kamas demonstratives exhibit a visible-nonvisible distinction.
Singular is unmarked.
The dual marker is -Gǝj. Kamas distinguishes dual possessive and personal suffixes, but there is no dual in absolute declension.

Kamas has two different nominal plural markers: 1) -jǝʔ (or -iʔ) in oblique case forms, and 2) SAŋ (-zaŋ ~ -zeŋ ~ -saŋ ~ -seŋ), which is the exclusive plural marker in the possessive declension and in the declension of demonstrative pronouns.
No information.
No information.
No information.

The head of a postpositional phrase is generally marked with the genitive case, but there are also primary postpositions that govern the lative or nominative case. Primary postpositions are uninflected, whereas secondary postpositions (i.e. grammaticalized nouns with local semantics) are inflected for case and, possibly, also for person.

The productive present tense marker in 19th- and 20th-century Kamas was -LiA. All verbs borrowed from Turkic form their present tense with this suffix. Its origin is most likely a construction involving the converb in -LAʔ and the copula verb i- in the zero tense (traditionally referred to as the ’aorist’ in Samoyedology), e.g., 1SG -LjA-m < *-LAʔ i-m. In addition to this form, there are three archaic present tense forms used only with specific verbs, as well as a newly developed durative present tense (continous form).
The marker is -BiA in 1st and 2nd person singular, and -Bi elsewhere.

The most frequent future tense marker is -LA. Less commonly, and only with transitive verbs, the marker-Lji occurs. The element -L- is the same as in the present tense marker -LiA and the converb ending -LAʔ, and most likely originates from the frequentative derivational marker -l.
Many different Aktionsarts (e.g., durative, resultative) represented under perfective and imperfective aspect, see Klumpp 2016:59)
Negation is marked either by a negation verb or by a negation particle.
The transitive marker is -Tǝ, non-productive marker for some verbs is -l.
For a reciprocal reading of the intransitivizer -Ō, a non-singular subject is required.
While in 3rd person present tense, zero-copula predication seems the default case, in 1st and 2nd person, zero-copula predication varies with predication with the help of the copula verb i- ‘be’.
The productive means of specifying imperfective and perfective Aktionsarten in Kamas is the use of auxiliaries that follow a converbal main verb.
In many cases, S is however realized by a zero pronoun.
SOV is the basic word order, with S in many cases realized by a zero pronoun. The order SVO occurs mainly in imperative clauses, but also in verum focus context.
In focus context.
The causative marker is -Tǝ.
There are three numbers in Kamas: singular, dual, and plural.
No information.
The comparative marker is -(a)rak, which is borrowed from Turkic and does not exhibit vowel harmony.
No information.
No information.
No information.
No information.
No information.
No information.
No information.

The parameters that trigger accusative marking of direct object expressions include definiteness, referentiality, givenness, and specificity. Obligatory direct object marking applies to formally definite object expressions.

The parameters that trigger accusative marking of direct object expressions include definiteness, referentiality, givenness, and specificity. Obligatory direct object marking applies to formally definite object expressions.

In Matthias Alexander Castrén’s mid-19th-century Kamas data, the marker -NTA could occur alone and denoted a (probably hypotetical) future tense, while the conditional/conjunctive meaning was contributed by the enclitic particle in its full form ize.
In definite conjugation.
In certain cases, there is a copula: in nɛŋ-en mil nǫmn wǫʌ (DET woman-2SG cap on.top be.PRS.3SG) ’This woman’s cap is on top’.
In traditional Khanty, coordinating conjunction is rare.
Usually the negative marker and the main verb are not separated, e.g. ʌapǝtmit săχ-ǝʌ aʌ ɛŋχ-e, …! (seventh fur.coat-3SG NEG.IMP take.on-SG<2SG) ’Don’t take on his seventh fur coat!’.

In the North Khanty nominal paradigm there are only NOM, LOC, LAT cases. Source is expressed with the postposition ewǝʌt 'from', e.g. cf. ŏsǝm iʌpijn 'inside the pillow', ŏsǝm iʌpija 'into the pillow' and ŏsǝm iʌpi ewǝʌt 'from the pillow'.
In the North Khanty nominal paradigm there are only NOM, LOC, LAT cases (e.g., χɔt house.NOM, χɔt-a house-LAT, χɔt-ǝn house-LOC). Regarding personal pronouns, there are NOM, ACC, DAT cases.
Case suffixes follow the number and possessive suffixes.
In the Kazym dialect, unlike in other North Khanty dialects, not only the quantity, but also the quality of the corresponding vowels are different.
In the Kazym dialect, unlike in other North Khanty dialects, not only the quantity, but also the quality of the corresponding vowels are different.
They are allophones, however, they are often differentiated in both the Cyrillic alphabet and in phonetic transcriptions.
ť is possible but rare.
Word-initial ŋ is prohibited.
In the Kazym Khanty dialect there is a distinction between these two sibilants. However, it is not true of all the northern dialects. In the northernmost one (Obdorsk Khanty), there is no š.
Only quantity phrases with a noun in the genitive singular or essive do not agree with verbal predicates.

Demonstrative pronouns do not agree in terms of case in the adnominal position when a noun is marked with a case other than comitative singular (but not plural), genitive, or nominative. Numerals from 'one' to 'six' demonstrate similar behavior. Demonstrative pronouns and numerals are marked with genetive, however, this form can be analyzed as an underspecified oblique form as well.
Non-finites require marking an S/A-argument with genitive or comitative.
Habitual and frequentative meanings are marked with the same derivational suffix.
Habitual and frequentative meanings are marked with the same derivational suffix.
Telic verbs in the present tense refer to the future. However, atelic verbs in the present tense can only refer to the future in the context of planned activities.
An inchoative marker can have prospective meaning only in some idiolects.
Perfect can sometimes have a mirative reading.
Epistemic possibility was encoded with potential mood in archaic language.
There were jussive forms in archaic language.
When used adnominally, the non-finite form on -əm <-эм> relativizes mostly non-core participants, however, occasionally it can relativize subject and object as well.
Szabó (1985) provides some examples of tentative possessive suffixes attached to participles (third person only), however, the interpretation of these examples remains somewhat doubtful.
There is a marker that can be used to express politeness, but it is not a dedicated morpheme (it takes on special connotations in certain communicative situations).
The agreement of adjectives in number has almost been lost in modern language.
The only adposition allowing a person marker is mʲiːlʲːtʲe 'along, behind, with'. However, person marking in this adposition has almost been lost in modern language.
Only genetive marking is possible in attributive constructions. External and predicative possessors require locative.
The use of mʲiː 'what' and kuː 'which' is more common, but kʲeː 'who' can sometimes still be used for inanimate objects as well.
Number and case are marked comulatively within a case-number marker.

There is a strict animacy distinction for the concepts 'new' and 'young'. For the concept 'old', there is a syncretic term wuəmʲː 'old (animate/inanimate)' alongside a specialized term puərʲesʲ 'old (animate); elder'.
There is the distinction only for 'warm':pɒːhk ‘warm (of the atmosphere)’ vs lʸiːŋʸgʸ-esʸ ‘warm (of the liquids)’.
Trisyllabic dactyl feet are also possible.
The comitative plural and inchoative markers require secondary stress, otherwise three or four syllabic forms do not obligatory have a secondary stress on the third syllable.

There are two lowering diphthongs:<уэ> /uə/ and <уа> /oa/ traditionally analyzed as /ua/. Some descriptions (e.g. Rießler 2022) analyze <е̄> and <я̄> as /ie/ and /ea/ respectively. In the modern language, /ie/ and /ea/ became monophthongs. Coarticulations triggered by palatalization can cause a diphthong-like effect.
Word-initial consonant clusters are restricted to recent Russian loanwords.
There was palatal [ʎ] opposed to palatalized [lʲ] in more archaic dialects. Nowadays, a cluster /lʲj/ is attested on the place of [ʎ].
Word-initial /b/, /d/, /g/ are restricted to functional words and recent borrowings.
[h] is analyzed as part of preaspiration occlusives in some descriptions (e.g.; Rießler 2022).
[ɣ] is attested in place of the modern word-final and word-medial [g] in more archaic dialects.
[ð] is attested in place of the modern word-final and word-medial [d] in more archaic dialects.

There is no phonemic opposition /w/ vs /v/. [w] and [v] are in free variation, at least in some positions. It is difficult to determine which label - /w/ or /v/ - is more appropriate for this phoneme.
/f/ is a rare, but absolutely full-fledged phoneme.
Kert (1971: 173) describes a more complicated system, but it is completely lost in modern Kildin Saami.
The distribution of -ə and zero markers (+ weak grade) is almost entirely phonological, there is only some small deviations.
There is no productive morpheme for plural marking. Most nouns mark plural with a weak grade. However, in a relatively small class of nouns, the plural is marked with -ə.
No arguments can be presented to define the direction of derivation (verb > noun or noun > verb) in the modern Kildin Saami, e.g. ɫaːwːɫ 'song' : ɫaːwːɫ-ə 'sing-INF'.
Present tense is marked cumulatively with person and number.
Past tense is marked cumulatively with person and number.
Only the verb 'to be' allows the morphological marking of the future tense, other verbs use different periphrastic constructions.
There is a polysemy of reflexive and reciprocal meaning.
The standard negation marker only occurs clause-initially when a subject is omitted.
There is a special converb to mark simultaneous action, but its use is not obligatory.
There is agreement only in the predicative position.
Constructions with floating qauntifier are common as well.
Special word order (V1) is accompanied by special intonation.
The comparison standard is marked with the partitive case.
There are some examples in the corpus, but it is a marginal strategy.
It is one of possible readings of perfect.
This is one of several strategies. A possessive suffix may be accompanied by a personal pronoun in the genitive, or the possessor may be marked only by a personal pronoun or a noun in the genitive.
In a special case, when the subject in singular is semantically in plural.
Agreement occurs in some cases.
Object marking depends on the animateness and definiteness.
In addition to nominative also partitive can be used to mark the subject.
Derivative suffix -ovlyny.
There are future markers in the 3rd person only.
The respective marker (potential) has been lost.
Reflexivity is expressed by means of a separate word.
Only in negative forms of the II (evidential) Past
It is, however, common to use se 'this'.
A separate operator is used for the present tense.
Both OV and VO are possible.
An analythic superlative is used as well.
There is syncretism between the illative and inessive in the possessive declention.
Due to Russian influence, a plural form is typical nowdays.
The demonstrative stem сiя is the same as the 3rd person pronoun.
Many affixes take stress, but not because they are heavy syllables.
Primary stress can fall on many derivational suffixes.
There are some exceptions, e.g., юöр 'message'.
Only in loan words, descriptive words, and few exceptions.
It is found in new loanwords (Lytkin 1962: 74), e.g., улица 'street'
Only in loanwords.
Only in loanwords.
Marked with Px (cf. GB065).
Marked with Px (cf. GB065).
0 for giving an approximate number
In case of proper names, plural marker takes this function.
Only 3SG and 3PL can be marked for the future tense.
Only for the verb 'to be'.
Future reference can be expressed with auxiliaries such as пондыны ‘will; start’ and мöдны ‘will; begin’.
Two verbs can be coupled to express the same thing from different angles: one verb expresses an action, the other the nature of the action or state.
Adjectives and ideophones with verbs видзны 'to hold', керны 'to do', мунны 'to go'.
Negation appears clause-initially if it is in focus.
However, the same negator (abu) is used for these three kinds of non-verbal negation and only for the 2nd past.
The passive meaning is found in a construction with a noun in the inctrumental case. It's rarely used.
Marked with REFL
There are simultaneous and sequential converbs.
Instrumental is used for this
The reflexive pronoun in accusative is used for this.
Imperative mood
The adnominal property word and the head noun do not show agreement.
Object marking depends on the animateness and definiteness.
In the past and future tense.
However, in the case of 3rd person, the verb appears in the future tense.
The particle эcкьö [eɕkɘ] is used.
The particle эcкьö [eɕkɘ] is used.
Usually the particle мед [med] + future form is used.
The negative participle -тöм [-tɘm] and the negative converb тöг [-tɘg] are used.
The converb in -ана [-ana] is relatively new in Komi, and it is more used in literature.
There is syncretism in the possessive declension.
There are only cases, which do not form a set.
There is a separate instrumental case (see also UT093).
Either the instrumental or nominative case is used.
There is possessive declension.
The order is not fixed (it depends on a case).
Only in some dialects.
Only in loan words and descriptive words.
Only in new loanwords, e.g. цемент [t͡sement] 'cement'.
Only in new loanwords, e.g. вахта [vɑхtɑ] 'watch'.
Only in new loanwords, e.g. фен [fen] 'blow dryer'.
Can be marked with possessive suffixes (see also GB023).
Inversion may be in poetic language.
There is only proximal vs. distal distinction: тайӧ 'this (here)' vs. distal сійӧ 'that (there)'.
There is only proximal vs. distal distinction: тайӧ 'this (here)' vs. distal сійӧ 'that (there)'.
There is only proximal vs. distal distinction: тайӧ 'this (here)' vs. distal сійӧ 'that (there)'.
Simple plural marker can take this function.
Usually formed with -ан. Some formations with -ӧм exist, but the latter suffix is not productive in this function.
Typical case alignment: agent: nominative; direct object: nominative or accusative; indirect object: dative. In certain situations, other cases are also used.
Typical case alignment: agent: nominative; direct object: accusative; indirect object: dative. In certain situations, other cases are also used.

-а- is the non-past tense marker for 1st and 2nd person of both numbers; in 3rd person, present and future are distinguished, -ӧ- being the present tense marker. Negation verb uses a separate stem to denote non-past tense.
Only 3SG and 3PL can be marked for the future tense with the -а-; other personal endings containing this morpheme can be used to express either present or future, with no formal distinction.

The so-called second, resultative, or evidential past tense, can only be used to refer to perfective actions. Additionally, verbal suffixes such as-ывл, -ывт, -ӧст, -л, -ысь can indicate completed actions, although in their case this meaning is implied and secondary rather than denoted.
Morphonologically conditioned в:л alternation or stem contractions may be observed in imperative and connegative forms.
Only for the verb 'to be'.
Suffixes -ӧд-, -т/д- is/are used for this purpose. Alternations are possible in roots whose original final consonant clusters have been simplified in their intransitive forms.
Formed with -зь/сь, -ч-.
Formed with -зь/сь, -ч-.
Imperatives of the verb вай can be used to mark 1PL imperative/hortative (вай тӧдмасям! вайӧй ветлам!).

Fossilised forms of the verb вӧвны 'to be' are used to mark continuous or habitual aspect along with past tense (explicit or evidential) on verbs. As they do not inflect for person, number or negation, they do not trigger 1 for this feature.
The verbs пондыны and кутны are used as auxiliaries marking future reference.
Verbs of similar or complementary meaning can be coupled to express a generalisation or to signal a greater scope of the activity (habituality, engagement of the actor in the process, etc.).
Coincides with predicate copula in tenses other than present and in negations in all tenses.
The basic word order is SVO or SVX, both in main and subordinate clauses.
Negation appears clause-initially if it is in focus.
-ś/ć-, in addition to being a reflexive/reciprocal suffix, is also used to form passive verbs.
-ś/ć-, in addition to being a reflexive/reciprocal suffix, is also used to form antipassive verbs.
There is distinction between simultaneous and sequential converbs.
Formed with -ӧд-. The subject of a transitive verb becomes the indirect object of a ditransitive verb.
Adjectives and adverbs can be reduplicated to express high intensity of the denoted trait. Numerals are reduplicated to form distributives.
-тор is productive as a diminutive suffix (Lytkin 1955:156), -ук is another diminutive suffix, although its usage is limited to personal names.
When nominalised with the definitive/possessive suffix and used in adposition, it can appear on either side of the described NP.
A possessor is in GEN.
Russian-borrowed али, разьве and неужели are used word-initially to form polar questions.
Elative is used for comparison.
Besides the elative case, postpositions дорысь and серти can be used to mark comparison, but the former also has locational meaning.
The Russian-loaned particle бӧльӧ can be used in colloquial speech.
All ideophones are characteristically reduplicated with only partial identity of the two parts. Their usage tends to be limited to qualifying a closed set of verbs.
Negation is marked by the particle abu in the 2nd past tense.
Instrumental can be used for this purpose, although there is a tendency to avoid it.
There is a reflexive pronoun, declined for all persons and cases, including core argument cases.
Imperative mood has distinct forms of the negation verb and compulsory -ӧй ending in all plural forms (including the 2PL connegative). Second past tense could be interpreted as evidential.
Second past tense (with forms in -ӧм-) is used to express evidentiality. The auxiliary вӧлӧм in compound past tenses can also convey this meaning.
Separate names for 20, 30, 40, ... and for each order of magnitude starting with 10^2.
Definite objects acquire accusative.
Indefinite objects take the accusative case form, wich has a zero morpheme.
The conjunction мый is used. Russian-loaned что is also found, although it is rarer and proscribed.
The postposition йылысь can be used to introduce a complement of a verb of knowing, although it introduces a topic, not object of knowing (in this sense, it is equivalent to the English 'about').

Fossilised forms вӧлі and вӧлӧма of the verb вӧвны 'to be' are used to mark continuous or habitual aspect of past tense (explicit or evidential respectively) with verb forms of present or future tense. In these constructions the semantic verb that declines for person, number and negation; вӧлі/вӧлӧма are undeclineable so they qualify as particles.

Fossilised forms вӧлі and вӧлӧма of the verb вӧвны 'to be' are used to mark continuous or habitual aspect of past tense (explicit or evidential respectively) with verb forms of present or future tense. In these constructions the semantic verb that declines for person, number and negation; вӧлі/вӧлӧма are undeclineable so they qualify as particles.
In 3Pl forms, the impersonal form is used.
Verbs have a separate reflexive paradigm.
The respective coordinating conjunction is da 'and'.
The neutral word order is VO.
It is possible only if the object is stressed/in focus.
The possessive suffixes are only used in a limited context (e.g. with kinship terms).
Although it is possible to add -päi in certain cases (e.g. školalpäi), such examples are not included here as -päi is no case marker on its own.
A postpositional construction is used instead.
Location and source are marked the same way.
In addition to the suffix -l also -lpäi may be used.
There are external and internal local cases.
Possessor is marked with the genitive.
The possessive suffix follows the case suffix.
The order is number + case marker.
The phonemic length distinction only applies to i, y, and u.
However, not necessarily.
Possible but infrequent.
However, further research is needed.
There are no articles
It seems possible but further research is needed.
There is no dual.
There is no trial.
There is no paucal.

Restricted use of possessive suffixes. Only used with words indicating close family relationships and only in the singular. Instead of possessive suffixes, also the genitive forms of the personal pronouns can be used.
Following the Russian model, anna 'give, let' may be used.
Possible, but rare.
Both nominative and partitive case can be used to express the subject.
Negation is formed using a negative particle.
Conditional mood is used instead.
Conjunctions i and da are used.
Such constructions can hardly be attested.
Cf. UT062.
However, piäл may also be used in the ablative function.
The accusative case is used only with pronouns.
Prolative forms are used only infrequently, e.g. meca-či (forest-PROL) 'through the forest'.
However, pihal can mark location as well as source.
However, there is overlap in goal and location marking.
See also UT096.
See also UT095.
A postposition is used (niškoi ~varoin 'for').
The use of possessive suffixes is rare; they are mainly attested in folksongs and with words denoting close relatives.
Palatal vowel harmony is, however, irregular, e.g. ńeičükät ~ ńeičukät girl.PL
Cf. hall [halː] ‘frost’
Only in loanwords, e.g. cuar´ [t͡suarʲ] 'czar', revolucaid revolution.PRT.SG
Only quantitative consonant gradation (see UT165).
However, further research is needed.

Dual is not marked on nominals (other than personal pronouns, reflexive pronouns and reciprocal pronouns). Dual is however marked on verbs, meaning that a subject in the plural may combine with a predicate in the dual.
There are several derivational suffixes, e.g. -addat.
However, that this does not apply to all dialects.

There is a conditional marker lulu- which is used with the infinitive (or perfect participle) of a verb (e.g. luluv låhkåt COND.1SG read.INF 'I would read'). However, this marker is probably best analyzed as a defective verb, and is hence not considered "morphological marking" here.
The conditional can be used in counterfactual conditional clauses (cf. however UT026).
There is the third person imperative.
ja 'and' is used for NPs, VPs and clauses
The abessive form of the verb can be used.
For example, the present participle is specialized to relativize S/A arguments.
Adpositions can have person/number marking, but this is optional and infrequent.

In a NP in the nominative, a noun following a numeral higher than 1 is usually analyzed as being in the genitive singular. However, from a synchronic perspective, the genitive singular and nominative plural are syncretic, meaning that an analysis of bárne as boy.NOM.PL would be equally valid.
The order is stem + case + px.
Standard Lule Saami reveals such distinction. In some varieties however, no age distinction is made.
A few derivational affixes carry stress, e.g. the inchoative -goahtet.

There is a phonetic contrast between [æ] and [e], but there is no phonological opposition as they are in complementary distribution, e.g. [e] in germaj snake.NOM.SG vs. [æ] in gärmmaha snake.GEN.SG can be analysed as allophones of the same phoneme.
Cf. nuorra /nuorra/ 'young'.
Cf. manen /manien/ 'why'.
There is no marker in the nominative plural, but in all other cases, the element -(i)j- is clearly visible in the plural forms.
It is difficult to differentiate between the person, number, and tense markers, but the element -i(j)- is clearly visible in the past-tense forms (Ylikoski_Lule 2022: 138-139).
The original potential mood suffix is used to mark the future (Ylikoski_Lule 2022: 139).
The inflectional conditional forms have largely fallen out of use. A periphrastic conditional consisting of the auxiliary lulu- ‘would‘ and the infinitive in -t is used instead.
VSO is possible for focusing purposes.
The inclusory construction is not mentioned in the literature (Spiik 1989, Ylikoski_Lule 2022), but it can be found in corpus data (SIKOR).
In the standard language, there is goabbák/guhtik + guojmme + PX (lit. ‘either/which + partner’)
The possessor is in the genitive case, but there is no overt grammatical suffix marking it.
Some nouns (e.g. kinship terms) can take possessive suffixes, but such occurrences are relatively infrequent (Ylikoski_Lule 2022: 136).

“On occasion, a grammatically singular noun that refers to a group of sorts (e.g., калык [kɑlək] ‘people’, кӱтӱ [kyty] ‘herd’, еш [jeʃ] ‘family’), or pair nouns/collective nouns [...], co-occur with plural forms of verbs. In these cases, singular verbal forms are however also possible, and recommended by prescriptive grammarians.”
There is no case agreement within noun phrases at all.
There is no case agreement within noun phrases at all.

“The gerunds in -мек(е) [-mek(e)] and -меш(ке) [-meʃ(ke)] take possessive suffixes; their agent, which can (but does not have to) differ from the agent of the superordinate (often finite) verb, can be marked by a preceding pronoun or noun. If the agent of the gerundial action is a noun, it can be in the nominative or the genitive. Inanimate nouns generally occur in the nominative, while animate nouns occur in either the nominative or genitive. When used with a genitive form, the gerund usually has a possessive suffix third person.” (Riese et al. 2019:254) The same applies to participles (EG 261, 265, 266); the picture is more complicated with postpositions (Riese et al. 2019:160) where the government depends on the individual postposition.
Differential object marking can be attested only in non-finite clauses (Riese et al. 2019:70).

Compound past tenses I & II (formed by present-tense form followed by ‘to be’ in the third person singular, either in simple past I or II) are habitual (the distinction between them is evidential). Also, aspectual auxiliaries (light verbs) илаш ‘to live’ and шогалаш ‘to stand up’ can be coupled with a converb and give the resulting pairing a habitual value.

Numerous defivational suffixes, descending by their productivity: v-л [-l]: лӱмдаш [lymdɑʃ] ‘to name’ → лӱмдылаш [lymdəlɑʃ] ‘to call names’, -ешт [-eʃt]: колаш [kolɑʃ] ‘to hear’ → колышташ [koləʃtɑʃ] ‘to listen’, -кал [-kɑl]: ойыраш [ojərɑʃ] ‘to separate’ → ойыркалаш [ojərkɑlɑʃ] ‘to sort’, -ед [-ed]: пуаш [puɑʃ] ‘to give’ → пуэдаш [puedɑʃ] ‘to hand out’. Also auxiliary опташ ‘to place (several items)’.
Aspectual auxiliary (light verb) илаш [ilɑʃ] ‘to live’ is used.
Especially in combination with atelic verbs or verbal pairings, the verb тӱҥалаш [tyŋɑlɑʃ] ‘to begin’ is used as a future auxiliary.
Simple past tense 1 forms can, in a structure presumably borrowed from Russian, be used to denote something that is about to happen in the immediate future.

The usual way of expressing this is using the converb of prior action. Also simple past III & IV (distinction between them is evidential), formed by a simple past II form followed by a past-tense form (simple past I and II respectively) of the verb ‘to be’ in the third person singular, are “used to denote an action in the past that preceded another action, or was interrupted by it”.

The particle улмаш [ulmɑʃ] (‘to be’ in simple past II, 3SG) is placed after verbs in sentences in order to “to imply an uncertainty of sorts, or an inference, and in some cases also that an event was unexpected and surprising”, e.g. паша-ште лий-м-ем годым тудо мал-а улмаш [pɑʃɑ-ʃte lij-m-em godəm tudo mɑl-ɑ ulmɑʃ] (work-INE be(come)-PTCP.PASS-PX1SG when 3SG sleep-3SG be.PST2.3SG) 'While I was at work, it turned out s/he was sleeping'. However, there is no grammatical disctinction between evidential and mirative form.
Only periphrastic forms are used.
There is a necessitive infinitive with the ending -мыла/-мӹлӓ [-məlɑ/-mə̈læ], and the future-necessitive participle in -шашлык/-шӓшлык [-ʃɑʃlək/-ʃæʃlək].
The future-necessitive participle in -шаш [-ʃɑʃ], which prototypically denotes necessity, can in some cases be used in this function.

There is, however, a suffix -ан [-ɑn] that can be attached to participles (Riese et al. 2019:253) to indicate that they denote possible states rather than real states, e.g. почмо [pot͡ɕmo] 'opened', почман [pot͡ɕmɑn] 'openable'.
Realized by a highly productive derivational suffix.
Probably Russian influence.
This typically happens in Mari (only) with causative verb forms. The verbs meaning ‘rain’, ‘snow’ , and ‘blow’ (wind) require a subject.
Copula is not needed, e.g. тудо пиалан [tudo piɑlɑn] (3SG happy) 's/he is happy'; it is, however, possible to use one.
The element -ды- [-də- ~ -tə-] is contained in numerous non-finite forms.
Realized by a highly productive derivational suffix.
While the participle is historically complex (negation element + ending of passive participle), it is treated as a unit in modern language.
