No agreement between modifiers and superordinate nouns in general; participles do not differ from this norm.
Works with all participles but the active participle.
The converb in -н, which overwhelmingly forms adverbial forms, forms a very small set of adjectives. This is not a productive process (Riese et al. 2019:269).
The basic word order is SOV. There is no obvious deviation from the norm in the imperative, at least not on a regular basis.

“The negation verb and the connegative form are tied to one another tightly in Mari. It is unusual for other elements (e.g., adverbs, pronouns) to occupy the space between them and it is unusual for the word order to change – the connegative form by default follows the negation verb.”
“Non-finite verbal forms are not inflected for tense and mood, but maintain some verbal properties, for example the argument structure (i.e., they can still have objects)."

Adjectives do not agree with the noun in number in the predicateve position, e.g. нине сӱрет-влак сылне ул-ыт [nine syret-vlɑk səlne ul-ət] (these picture-PL beautiful be-PRS.3PL) 'these pictures are beautiful'.
Analytic formation is used.

“Genitive forms, especially of pronouns, can be used as nominals meaning ‘my one, mine’, ‘your one, yours’, ‘his/her own one, his/hers’, etc. In this case, they can be declined as nouns. Other suffixes, also case suffixes, can follow the genitive suffix.”
The morphologization of certain postpositions (e.g. дек ‘to’) is proceeding faster on demonstrative pronouns: туддек ‘to him/her’ (Riese et al. 2019:136).
The nominative case is used on both occasions, e.g. пӧрт [pørt] 'house' кок пӧрт [kok pørt] 'two houses'.

The postposition деч [det͡ɕ] ‘from’ could be said to be a de-facto case for which the morphologization is complete; it is only description tradition and orthography that justifies its classification as a postposition rather than a case suffix (Riese et al. 2019:163-164)

The three postpositions - деке [deke] ‘to’ (Riese et al. 2019:169-170), дене [dene] ‘with’ (Riese et al. 2019:170-172), деч [det͡ɕ] ‘from’ (Riese et al. 2019:172-174) - used similar to external local cases in Finnic languages could be said to be de-facto cases for which the morphologization is complete; it is only description tradition and orthography that justifies its classification as a postposition rather than a case suffix (Riese et al. 2019:163-164).

The postposition дене [dene] ‘with’ could be said to be a de-facto case for which the morphologization is complete; it is only description tradition and orthography that justifies its classification as a postposition rather than a case suffix (Riese et al. 2019:163-164). NB! The so-called comitative case mentioned in Mari grammars and textbooks has a radically different function.

“The comparative case is used to express similarity, likeness and can usually be translated into English as ‘like something/someone’, ‘as something/someone’. The object/person with which a noun in the comparative is compared can be used in varying syntactic functions in the sentence: subject, object, etc.”.
The dative is used to mark benefactors.

It should be noted that inanimate nouns traditionally did not occur in the genitive at all; inanimate nouns serving as a modification do occur in the nominative (Riese et al. 2019:61-68), e.g. Вена университет [Venɑ universitet] (Vienna.NOM university) University of Vienna’ (see www.mari-language.com).
“Like the possessive suffix third person singular […] this possessive suffix can beused as a determining element, though its usage is more typical of folklore texts” (Riese et al. 2017:125).

The possessive suffix is used to indicate a rreference to something previously discussed or otherwise salient to the conversation. It is especially commonly used to refer to a subset of a larger whole that is salient.
The “degrees of freedom” depend on the case.

The same word is used in both cases, e.g. шокшо игечe [ʃokʃo iget͡ɕe] 'hot weather' - шокшо вӱд [ʃokʃo vyd] ‘hot water’; йӱштӧ игечe [jyʃtø iget͡ɕe] ‘cold weather’ - йӱштӧ вӱд [jyʃtø vyd] ‘cold water’.

Geminate (long) consonants only occur at morpheme boundaries. Minimal pairs are possible: ныллымше [nəlːəmʃe] ‘fortieth’ (← ныл [nəl] ‘four’ + лу [lu] ‘ten’ + ORD), нылымше [nələmʃe] ‘fourth’ (← ныл [nəl] ‘four’ + ORD).
Reduced vowels are shorter than full vowels, but there is a clear qualitative difference between non-reduced and reduced vowels, so it cannot be reasoned that vowel length alone is distinctive.
No distinctive vowel length at all.
No distinctive vowel length at all; geminate consonants only at morpheme boundaries.
However, as final unstressed [e] is reduced, it could be argued that the qualitative difference of this vowel is critical to the phonological disctinction between these words.
In Meadow Mari, suffixes containing full vowels are stressed, but this is in line with the regular stress rules.
The last full vowel (i.e. anything but [ə] or unstressed/reduced final [e], [o], [ø]) is stressed: пеледыш [peˈledəʃ] ‘flower’, пеледеш [peleˈdeʃ] ‘blossoms’.
Syllable structure has no transparent effect on where stress falls.
The last full vowel (i.e. anything but [ə] or unstressed/reduced final [e], [o], [ø]) is stressed.
Only for labial vowels.
Further palato-velar distinction for labial vowels.
There is however a “mid unrounded central vowel, reduced vowel similarly to the English reduced vowel that is denoted in different ways (alone, lemon, heaven)”, e.g. пыл [pəl] ‘cloud’.
Fairly rare and the effect of a fairly recent loss of reduced vowels that did not happen in all varieties of Mari.
The palatal consonants [ʎ] and [ɲ] are frequently mislabelled as palatalized. Palatalized pronunciation under Russian influence and especially in Russian loan words does happen.
Only in Russian loan words among contemporary speakers.
Only in Russian loan words.

Historically as allophones of the fricatives /β/, /γ/, /δ/ after homorganic nasals, i.e.: [mb], [ŋg], [nd]. “The historical pronunciation of these sounds is encountered only rarely today: [β] has generally been replaced by [v]; /b/ is perceived as an independent phoneme; the sounds [γ] and [δ] are mainly realized as [g] and [d] in all positions.”

It is uncommon, but not excluded. There are some words of proto-Uralic origin that have an initial r. There are some varieties in which intial r is preceded by prothetic ə, however, presumably under Chuvash indluence.

“Historically, Mari had three voiced fricative phonemes: /β/, /γ/ and /δ/ […] The historical pronunciation of these sounds is encountered only rarely today: […] the sounds [γ] and [δ] are mainly realized as [g] and [d] in all positions.”
Only in Russian loan words.

“Historically, Mari had three voiced fricative phonemes: /β/, /γ/ and /δ/ […] The historical pronunciation of these sounds is encountered only rarely today: […] the sounds [γ] and [δ] are mainly realized as [g] and [d] in all positions.”
“Historically, Mari had three voiced fricative phonemes: /β/, /γ/ and /δ/ […] The historical pronunciation of these sounds is encountered only rarely today: [β] has generally been replaced by [v]”.
Only in Russian loan words.

There is alternation between [t] and [d] (not marked in orthography; д is used regardless of pronunciation) that has been said to resembles consonant gradation or final consonant devoicing: кид [kit] ‘hand’, кит-лан [kit-lɑn] hand-PX1PL, кид-ем [kid-em] hand-PX1SG. However, the historical explanation here is that [d] was spirantized to [ð] between vowels, and was later replaced by [d] under Russian influence.
Sometimes possessive suffixes can be used to indicate discourse-relevance.
In certain contexts, the numeral ик 'one' functions as a non-definite determiner.
In certain contexts, the numeral ик 'one' functions as a non-definite determiner.
Sometimes possessive suffixes can be used to indicate discourse-relevance.

The conjunctions да and ден can both be translated as 'and'. The former is universal and can be used for clause combining, the latter is reserved for nouns. When ден is used, unlike with да, only the last (latter) element bears a case marker.
There is only proximal vs. distal distinction: тиде 'this (here)' vs. distal тудо 'that (there)'; a rare pronoun саде 'that (aforementioned)' is used comparatively rarely in an anaphoric sense.
There is only proximal vs. distal distinction: тиде 'this (here)' vs. distal тудо 'that (there)'; a rare pronoun саде 'that (aforementioned)' is used comparatively rarely in an anaphoric sense.
There is only proximal vs. distal distinction: тиде 'this (here)' vs. distal тудо 'that (there)'; a rare pronoun саде 'that (aforementioned)' is used comparatively rarely in an anaphoric sense.

The plural markers are -влак, -шамыч, and -ла. They are not mutually exclusive and the choice is not lexically conditioned. The variation between -влак, -шамыч, historically dialectal, is a matter of stylistic choice in the literary language. The suffix -ла is mostly reserved for usage with temporal or spatial semantics.

The canonical nominaliser suffix is -маш, though forms with the passive participle suffix -м(е/о/ӧ) are very often converted to action nouns, in the process becoming neutral with regard to voice. The negative counterpart of -маш, expressing the state of 'not doing something', is -дымаш.
The suffixes -(ы)ш and -тыш can be used for this purpose, but neither of them is fully productive.

There are two past tenses. The first past tense is, depending on the verb's conjugational class, marked by palatalisation of a stem-final л /l/ or н /n/ before a vowel (no marking in stems which do not end in /l/ or /n/ or in 1PL and 2PL where the stem is followed by a suffix starting with a consonant), or by the suffix -ш. The second past tense is marked by -ын or -ен, again depending on the conjugational class. Additionally, the first past tense has a dedicated negation auxiliary.

Present-future tense forms of the copula verb лияш 'to be; to become' are used to express future states, as opposed to the other copula, улаш 'to be', whose respective forms refer rather to the present.
Though there exist a number of aspectual derivational suffixes, perfective and imperfective are not distinguishable on the verb within its paradigm.
Mari uses only an extremely limited number of prefixes, none of which belong to verbal morphology, and no proclitics at all.
Mari uses only an extremely limited number of prefixes, none of which belong to verbal morphology, and no proclitics at all.
Mari uses only an extremely limited number of prefixes, none of which belong to verbal morphology, and no proclitics at all.
The verb улаш 'to be' takes on a special 3rd person singular form уло when used as an existential copula, rather than the regular улеш, which is present in its paradigm as a standard copula.
Benefactive meaning can be expressed with the use of auxiliary constructions (Riese et al. 2022:290-291).
However, in dialects, accusative can be used in both cases (see Saarinen 2022:465).
Directionality can be expressed with the use of auxiliary constructions (Riese et al. 2022:264-265).

Present-future tense forms of the copula verb лияш 'to be; to become' are used to express future states, as opposed to the other copula, улаш 'to be', whose respective forms refer rather to the present.

There are two conjugations, with distinct sets of (personal, stem, participle, etc.) suffixes following the stem. Although certain derivational suffixes can generally be associated with one or the other conjugation, in general the class of the verb is not predictable.
Several suffixes can be used for this purpose: -(ы)кт-, -т/д-, -тар/дар-.
Reflexives are formed with the -алт- suffix.
Sometimes frequentative markers can introduce a reciprocal reading, but this is not a productive phenomenon (Jeremy Bradley, p.c.).
Zero in 3SG present tense.
The function of this type of construction is fulfilled in Mari by auxiliary constructions consisting of a converbial (gerundial) form and a syntactically superordinate verb.

On top of dedicated auxiliaries semantically expressing modality, select verbs (шуаш, лияш) can be used in certain constructions as auxiliaries to introduce shades of modality: desiderative or potential (Riese et al. 2022:257-258,276-279.
The verb тӱҥалаш ('to begin') can be used to indicate future tense.
There are several examples of verb compounds, but their productivity calls for further research.
The verb ышташ 'to do; to make' would be the most likely candidate but most of its usage seems literal rather than as a light-verb.

In present tense, an irregular 3SG form уло of the verb улаш ('to be') is used in existential and possessive constructions. Otherwise, a zero-copula is used for 3SG. Negation in such clauses is expressed in the present tense with a particle уке, which is also different from the negation paradigm of the standard copula.
There is a distinction between standing, sitting and lying position.
The subject and the object precede non-finite verbal forms used for subordination, just as they precede finite verbs in unmarked clauses.
The clausal object precedes the verb, same as the nominal object.
The order is not fixed but relatively rigid.
Present-tense existential and possessive clauses use a special negator уке.
Certain verbs of feeling (e.g. кокырыкташ 'to make/have a cough', варныкташ 'to make/be sick', кылмыкташ 'to freeze, to feel cold') can be used impersonally with their experiencer in accusative.
The suffix -алт can be used to form, among others, verbs with a passive meaning.

Converbs are specialised to indicate temporal relations between events in clauses. Additionally, a series of Past Tense 1 forms is interpreted as a sequence of events, while a series of Past Tense 2 forms is read as simultanous events.
Several suffixes can be used for this purpose: -(ы)кт-, -т/д-, -тар/дар-.
Several patterns of reduplication are used to intensify adjectives and adverbs: repetition, partial reduplication and reduplication of the first CVC segment as Cvp.
There are no noun classes/genders in Mari.
There are no noun classes/genders in Mari.
There are no noun classes/genders or articles in Mari.
The proximal demonstrative appears in its plural form нине before nouns morphologically marked as plural. The plural form нуно of the distal demonstrative is generally used as a modifier.
There are no articles.
There are no noun classes/genders in Mari.
There are no genders in Mari.
There are no genders in Mari.
There are no noun classes/genders in Mari.

All – чыла, every – кажне; both precede their nouns. The word еда also translates into English as 'every' and follows the noun it modifies but its meaning is local/temporal and not universal, so it is not relevant here.
The possessor in a possessive construction is expressed with the genitive case, the same which is used adnominally.
Particles such as ма, мо can be used to mark polar questions, following a focused element or the main verb in clauses without focus.
There is no tone in Mari.
Most of them reduplicated 'pair words', e.g., лыб-лыб 'trembling, shaking', шыҥ-шыҥ 'crowded', выче-выче 'polite', йылт-йолт 'flashing, shining'.
The negation auxiliary (Riese et al. 2022:274-276) and contracted negated forms of the verb улаш 'to be' (Riese et al. 2022:292) cover the entire finite verb paradigm.
The verb 'to give' is a regular 2nd conjugation (-эм) verb.

Agents related to natural phenomena can be expressed with the postposition дене. Wider use of this construction to express animate agents is a rather recent and possibly unstable development, evidently under Russian influence.
The reciprocal pronoun икте-весе and its declined forms consist of elements meaning 'one' and 'the other'.

There are two morphological past tenses, of which one (PST1) is generally more often used to describe events seen firsthand or with a direct connection to the moment of speaking; these tend to, although do not necessarily need to be more recent than events described with the other past tense (PST2). Additional compound past tenses express background events, habituality, evidentiality, etc. — but not different remoteness in time in the strict sense.
There are dedicated personal markers for imperative, and a specialised morpheme -не- preceding personal markers and denoting desiderative mood.
The idiosyncratic forms мемнан ‘our’ and тендан ‘your (plural)’ in 1PL and 2PL contain an additional possessive suffixe.
No noun classes/gender exist in Mari.
No noun classes/gender exist in Mari.
No dual number in Mari.

-влак, considered a plural suffix by grammars, is atypical in that it does not take stress and there is a certain variation in placement of case suffixes when it is used; nevertheless, certain case suffixes will always follow it, and it doesn't have its own stress, which is enough to consider it phonologically bound.
No trial number in Mari.
No paucal number in Mari.
No noun classes/gender exist in Mari.
Direct evidence is unmarked in Mari.
Compound past tenses formed with the particle улмаш indicate second-hand information, reference or mirativity.
The class of question words does not include verbs.
Rare and usually proscribed.
The verb ужаш 'to see' is a regular 1st conjugation (-ам) verb.
The verb толаш 'to come' is a regular 1st conjugation (-ам) verb.
The complementizer нерген 'about' appears at the end of the dependent clause.
A possessor noun can be marked with the genitive case.
A possessed noun can be marked for the person/number of the possessor.

Айда, айста can introduce commands in first person plural but cannot be considered mood markers. Secondary past tenses formed with the particles ыле, улмаш can be used in conditional and counterfactual expressions but this is only one of their many applications.
The particles ыле, улмаш when used with non-past verb forms indicate a habitual action in the past; with past forms they indicate relatively more remote past.
The particles ыле, улмаш when used with non-past verb forms indicate a habitual action in the past; with past forms they indicate relatively more remote past.
In attributive clauses the modifier has no case/number marker.
PST2 expresses habituality.
In addition to expressing repeated events, PST2 can also express events that were continuing in the past.
The forms of present cover both present and future time reference.

Aspectual oppositions can be expressed with the objective vs. subjective conjugation. In the past tense, the negative marker takes person marking, and the two conjugation types are differentiated in the negative marker.
The infinitival participle -ma can mark necessity. The subject has to be in the dative case in this construction.
The passive/reflexive marker v is used to express capability, possibility, passive voice (depending on a context).
There is no reflexive conjugation, there is only the passive/reflexive marker v, e.g. käd'-ǝn-za tapa-v-s't' (hand-PL-POSS.3SG break-PASS-PST1.3PL) 'his/her hands got broken'.
In Moksha, 2nd and 3rd person plural is always distinguished: kunda-samas't' (catch-PRS.2PL>1SG/1PL) 'you catch me/us' and kunda-samaz' (catch-PRS.3PL>1SG/1PL) 'they catch me/us'.
It is also possible to use Russian loanword štoba 'in order to' + infinitive.
The copula is covert: son mokšа (3SG.NOM Moksha) 's/he is a Moksha'.
In the past tense, it is possible to use the copula with predicate nominals, but predicate nominals can also be conjugated in the second past tense.
In other persons the locative adverbial gets verbal endings: Mon kud-s-an (1SG house-INE-1SG) 'I am at home'.

Coordinating conjunctions are all of Russian origin (Hamari and Ajanki 2022:428); with nouns different types of coordinating is also possible: t'äd'ä-t-s't'iR'-t' (mother-PL.NOM-girl-PL.NOM) 'mothers and daughters'.

In existential sentences, the negation particle aš is used. In other sentences, the negation particle af is used in the present tense and the negation verb iz'- or aš- (which conjugates according to person) in the past tense.
In the present tense, negation is symmetric, e.g. koRt-an : af koRt-an (speak-PRS.1SG : NEG speak-PRS.1SG) 'I speak : I don't speak'.

Participle and gerund forms are negated with the negative construction apak + connegative (in Moksha the connegative with the participle apak ends in k, similar to the imperative of the 2nd person singular), instead of other constructions used with finite verbs.
The active present participle is used to relativize S/A.
The past participle and the gerund are syncretic only in the negated forms. In affirmative forms, there are different endings for the past participle and the gerund.
saJ-t' mar' (take-IMP.2SG apple) 'Take an apple'.
However, it is not very common.
In addition to the nominative, the partitive singular is also possible.
Both the singular and the plural is possible with numerals bigger than 1.

The abessive can be used as a case ending or in an adnominal function as an adjective. The latter function might be more frequent. Therefore, the abessive is not considered as a case ending in most of the grammars.
The inessive -sa expresses the instrumental as well: mašina-sa (car-INE) 'with a car'.
The dative is used (see UT082).
Moksha has three declension types: basic declension, definite declension (expresses that the referent of the noun is known to the speaker and the listener) and the possessive declension.
The 3SG possessive suffix can be used instead of the definite declension with some unique nouns, however, this use is restricted.
Not discussed.

The pronoun kijä 'who' is not used frequently, but the kona 'who, that, which' is used instead. It appears with animates: loman'-c'/buka-s', kona sa-s' c'ej (person-DEF.NOM/ox-DEF.NOM that come-PST1.3SG here) 'The person/ox that came here' as well as with inanimates valks-s', kona lis'-s' mel'ä (dictionary-DEF.NOM which come.out-PST1.3SG) 'the dictionary that came out last year'.
Kona 'who, that, which' is used with both animates and inanimates (see UT107).

The possessive suffixes usually follow the case suffixes; the genitive and dative suffixes are fused. However, in the case of 1Sg genitive forms the order could be rendered as px + GEN, i.e., ava-z'ǝ-n' (mother-POSS.1SG-GEN) 'my mother's (Niina Aasmäe, p.c. 2020).

The singular is unmarked, whereas in the plural the plural suffix t : t' (sometimes just the voicelessness of the previous consonant) follows the noun, and it is followed by the definiteness marker and the case ending.
There is only distinction for 'old', i.e. tašta 'old (inanimate)', s'ir'ä 'old (animate)'.
There is only distinction for cold: kel'mä ved' 'cold water', ušǝ-sa jakšama (outside-INE cold) 'it is cold outdoors'.

Geminate consonants only occur on morpheme boundary. They may arise when a consonant-initial suffix is attached to a consonant stem, e.g., (E) мaкссa mɑks-sɑ attached to a consonant stem, e.g., каннe- kɑnj-njǝ- (carry-FRQ-).
See UT125.
Word stress is relatively free and it does not distinguish between meanings.
The variants of the emphatic particle (clitic), -ga, -ge, -ka, -ke, -vok, -nga 'even, too': mon-ga 'me, too' tend to have primary or secondary stress.

The first syllable tends to be stressed if the word is uttered as a dictionary item; in speech, mostly disyllabic feet with a stressed and unstressed syllable alternate: MOn sǝrxk-An insAru - Mon sƏrxk-an InsarU 'I will go to Insar'. However, word stress can be subordinated to a rhythmical sentence stress.

A tendency to stress the non-first syllable is noticeable in words with the high vowels /i/ or /u/ in the first syllable and the low vowels /ɑ/ or /æ/ in a non-first syllable, e.g., śijÄ 'silver', kujA 'fat'. However, rhythmic initial-syllable stress functions in these words as well.

In Moksha the occurrence of /ɑ/, /æ/, and /ǝ/ in non-initial syllables is partly dependent on the vocalism of the first syllable; /ɑ/ occurs in the non-initial syllable when the first syllable has a back vowel and /æ/ when the first syllable has a front vowel. While systematic vowel harmony of suffixes does not exist (e.g., the inessive is consistently ‑sɑ), there are some relics of suffixal harmonization, e.g., кyдy kud-u (house-LAT) but вeли velj-i (village-LAT).
There are only monophtongs.
There are only monophtongs.
Before front vowels, labials and velars may be palatalized: ve 'night' > v'e, kev 'stone' > k'ev'.
Only in loanwords.
However, in Moksha native words /f/ occurs as a reflex of original /v/ before /t/, e.g., шуфта ʃuftɑ, cf. Erzya чувто t͡ɕuvto ‘tree’.
Marking of the direct object depends on the aspectual meaning expressed.
However, possible with ‘feed’.
The future tense can be expressed analytically using the verb karmams ‘start’.
The inchoative suffix -əźəv- indicates a lack of control over one's behavior.
suffix və̑ / və
Interrogative pronouns and indefinite quantifiers are also reduplicated.
śembə 'all', fśakaj 'all' require plural marking on the accompanying noun, while každaj / kažnaj 'every', eŕ 'every' require singular marking.
The demonstrative śä occurs in the ablative case, preceding the adjective.
Negation is marked by an inflecting word in the preterite tense and in the conjunctive, desiderative, imperative, and optative moods.
Negation is marked by a non-inflecting word in the present and 2nd preterite tenses and in the conditional and conditional-conjunctive moods.
Definite objects are marked by the definite genitive case (and postposition esa).

There is an agreement with the logical subject. In the comitative construction the agreement is dual (plural), however a few instances of singular, the verb is always in dual if the subject in a pronoun in dual. If there are two grammatical subjects (such as my mother and my father) in a sentence, the predicate is in the dual. This expresses that the two subjects act together. After quantifiers such as ŋukəgəə ‘many, much’, bǝnsǝ ‘all’ or numerals, the predicate agrees formally with the subject, i.e. it appears in the singular.
The predicate agrees in person and number with the subject.
Verbs in the objective conjugation agree with the object in number, the ending depends on the number. The verbal marker for object in dual is complex, it contains the dual marker.
Only the declination of three grammatical cases (nominative, accusative, genitive) can appear on adjectives. In all other cases, the pre-modifier is in the genitive case.
In all locative cases.
The subject of the temporal clause may be appear in the genitive.
With imperative forms the nominative is used.
In the active sentence, the over subject is always expressed with the nominative case.
The suffix -mUNHA shows that a situation takes place reguraly.
However, the imperfective verbs in the aorist tense refer to the present tense and thus express progressivity.
The resultative is used to indicate that an action will start immediately.
All tense markers can appear on the negative auxiliary.
The dubitative mood may be used to express surprise. There is also an exclamative clitic that has a mirative usage.
Optative may be used to express wishes or admonition. This mood is, however, rarely attested.
There is necessitive (or debitive mood), but it is only seldom used.
There is a speculative mood that is used to express speaker's assumptions.
Reflexive-medial conjugation shows the same person/number marking, e.g., ďeŋhiďi-Ɂi-ti is used for 2DU and 3DU (< ďembiɁsʲi 'to dress').
Passive sentences are formed using a passive suffix or a passive participle.
In generic statement, normally the 2nd person possessive suffix is used as subject marker.
Typically supine structures are used; the bare infinitive occurs with motion verbs.
Tense and mood markers only appear on verbal categories.
If the pivot is expressed by a pronoun, copula is not obligatory.
Conjunctive coordinated clauses are usually juxtaposed without any coordinator. However, there are also some borrowed coordinators. The Russian coordinator is only used at the clause level.
The abessive participle.
Passive structures can be formed from both transitive and intransitive verbs.
The abessive participle refers to actions that have not yet occurred.
However, the necessitative participle has also been treated as a future participle.
Participles show only partial agreement in case.
However, there is a separate suffix to indicate professioals, e.g. basu-Ɂsʲi ‘professional hunter’.
The negative verb must precede the main verb, e.g., ŋanuďətə ŋəmsu ŋuəi-kalʲi-Ɂ ńi-Ɂ mətu-Ɂ [ordinary meat one-CAR-ADV NEGAUX-IMP.2SG cut-CNG] ‘Don’t cut an ordinary meat!’
However, there is a suffix that can be used in the attenuative meaning (i.e. to do something a bit).
Analytic constructions are used.
E.g., lative: ńi ‘onto’, locative: ńini, elative: ńitə, prolative: ńimənɨ.
Plural or dual is rarely used.
The lative is also used to express the recipient and the beneficiary.
Prolative is used to mark direction.
The suffix has been recently developed from the lative postposition na.
With paired body parts, dual is typically used. In modern texts also plural can be found.
The non-pronominal possessor appears in the genitive case, whereas the pronominal possessor occurs in the nominative case (see also UT102).
The nominative form is used with pronouns.
The possessive suffixes refer to the person and number of the possessor but also the number of the possessed.
The referential use of 2Sg possessive suffix may occur only when the marked NP occurs in the subject position (see also UT105).
Possessive suffixes can be used in the referential function that is similar to the definite article but it cannot be argued that the language has definite articles.
The distinction is made between humans vs. non-humans. However, in certain cases, 'what' can also be used for a human referent.
There are no relative pronouns.
There are no relative pronouns.
In plural, the number marker and the case suffix are fused.
There is a binary opposition regarding vowel length; phonologically long vowels constitute two syllables.
The maximal syllable is CV͡VC; any vowel or diphtong can appear in the nucleus position.
Primary stress falls on the penultimate syllable.
However, words ending in long vowels or vowel sequences trigger stress.
It is possible if a word is trisyllabic or longer.
Occurs with limitations.
Occurs with limitations.
Occurs with limitations.
Two diphthongs that have phonemic status: /i͡a/ and /u͡a/. However, they have also been interpreted as monophthongs (see Janhunen and Gruzdeva 2020).
The palatal stop [c] occurs as an allophone of /t͡ʃ/.
Only /b/ and /g/, whereas /d/ appears as an allophone of /nt/ and in some Russians loanwords. However, see UT162 for a comment by Janhunen and Gruzdeva (2020).
/Ɂ/, /j/, and /r/ are not allowed in word-initial position.
However, according to Janhunen and Gruzdeva (2020), the intervocalic spirant [ð] and the postnasal voiced stop [d] represent allophones of a single phoneme that could be rendered as /d/.
No proper consonant geminates at all.
The PL marker can have associative reading in some contexts.
No gender.
No gender.
No gender.
No gender.
NOM = ACC.
No prefixes in the language.
Infixiation mentioned, but no examples for verbs given.
Their aorist suffix will differ: for perfective verbs - -Ɂə, for imperfective - NTU.
Destinative/benefactive is marked on the nouns or demonstrative pronouns in the beneficiary or destinative constructions.
Instrumental marked on nouns.
Destinative/benefactive, but no directional.
Nganasan has a separate reflexive conjugation, i.e. no dedicated reflexive marker.
A transitive verb can be formed from an intrasitive verb with a transitive-causative suffixes -BTU and -RU.
No gender.
No gender.
No gender.
No articles.
No grammatical gender.
The quantifiers differ in form: bǝnsǝ 'all; whole', bǝńd'ika 'every; all of them'.
Verbless interrogative sentences have a high-pitch intonation pattern, the tone peek being higher than that of an affirmative sentence.
Direct evidentiality is unmarked.
There are two interrogative quantifiers kanə 'how many, which' and kaŋkə 'which, how much'.
Cardinal numerals are compounded in numbers 11 and above. Between 11 and 19 the structure of the numerals is "10 + 1, 2 …". Numerals ending in 0 are also compounded: 20 is 2 + 10 etc.
The personal pronouns have the same form in the role of an object and subject.
The adnominal property word agrees with the head noun in all cases.
The negation is formed using a negative particle.
Potential is not used very often.
Typically, the conditional mood is used.
It is common to use the copula, e.g., Mie ole-n pihalla (I be-1SG outside) 'I'm outside'.
There are separate action nominalizations, e.g., šynny-ntä (< šyntyö) 'birth', juo-mini (< juuvva) 'drinking'.
Additionally, pronouns appear in the accusative case.
Zaikov (2013), however, considers šuati as postposition.
The postposition myötiä 'along' is used instead.
The locative case shows syncretism with the lative case, e.g. štola-lla (table-ADE/ALL) 'to the table, on the table'.
There is syncretism between goal and location marking.
The comitative is added to the plural stem but it can either have a singular or plural interpretation, cf. venehe-llä (boat-ALL/ADE) 'by boat'.
A postposition is used instead, e.g., šilma varoin paiššetty (you.PRT for bake-PASS.PST.PTCP) 'baked for you'.
case suffix + possessive suffix
Only in the case of i, y and u.
ŋ is an allophone, e.g., kenkät [ˈkeŋ.kæt] 'shoes'
Cf. vasara, sakara (also in Tver) (see Zaikov 2013).
In recent loanwords only, e.g., balalaikka.
Numeral comes before a noun.
Adnominal demonstrative comes before a noun.
However, not necessarily.
However, further research is needed.
There are no articles.
Adnominal property word comes before a noun.
The syncretic adessive-allative case has both locational and dative functions.
Studies and descriptions not sufficient.
At least sporadic examples can be found.
There are four moods.
There is no dual.
There is no trial.
There is no paucal.
Further research required.
The possessor noun is in the genitive case.
However, no possessive suffixes with 1Pl and 2Pl.
Following the Russian model, anna 'give, let' may be used.
With the 3rd person plural verb forms, omitting the subject results in a passive reading.
Finite verbs agree in number and person with the subject.
The verb not only indicates that the object is determinate but also encodes the number of the object (SG, PL, DU).
Attributive adjectives are not inflected for case or number.
Attributive adjectives are not inflected for case or number.

Source_Comment: Jeremy Bradley, Csilla Horváth, and Elena Skribnik (p.c. 2020)
Jeremy Bradley, Csilla Horváth, and Elena Skribnik (p.c. 2020)

Comment: See also UT017.
See also UT016.
There is no separate future tense in Mansi, the present tense may also get future interpretation in a proper context.
The respective meaning is usually conveyed by the verb in the present tense form + context.
Tense is marked on the lexical verb.

In Northern Mansi, mirative forms have evolved through desubordination, thus, there is reason to regard mirativity as a separate category. The category of evidentiality and, accordingly mirativity, has different origins in Khanty dialects.

For the 1st person the particle wos ~ os with indicative present may be used. However, -nuw, conventionally labelled as a conditional marker, is also used as an optative marker, e.g., jomas jurt wēk χōnt-nuw-əm [good friend only find-COND-1SG] 'If only I found a good friend / I would want to find a good friend'.

The 'if'-clause does not contain conditional marker (see Riese 2001:48), e.g., at ke jursʲ-al-as-lən manar-e al-nuw-uw [NEG if team_up-MOM-PST-SG<2SG what-SG<3SG kill-COND-1PL] ’If you wouldn't have taken me as a companion, what would have we hunted?’ (OUBD 1235:231).
The volitional particle wos(s) 'let' + indicative non-past form can be used.
The volitional particle wos(s) 'let' + indicative non-past form can be used.
There is syncretism between 2SG ~ 2DU ~ 2PL possessive suffixes, e.g., śōpit-as-aγən repair-PST-DU<2SG ~ repair-PST-DU<2DU ~ repair-PST-DU<2PL.

Source_Comment: Jeremy Bradley, Csilla Horváth, and Elena Skribnik (p.c. 2020)

Comment: There are no original coordination conjunctions in Mansi; NPs have their own coordinating strategies (e.g., dual or plural forms or the particle os 'also'); VPs and clauses are connected through juxtaposition or bridging linkage (based on non-finite clause combining). Recently the Russian i 'and' is borrowed - and the functions of os 'also' are extended to clauses.
Source_Comment: Jeremy Bradley, Csilla Horváth, and Elena Skribnik (p.c. 2020)

Comment: There is a separate element for existential negation – āt'im.
Negation is symmetric.

Source_Comment: Jeremy Bradley, Csilla Horváth, and Elena Skribnik (p.c. 2020)

Comment: The suffix -tāl ~ -tal is used to make privative adjectives from nouns and privative participles from verbs.
Intransitive verbs, including meteorological, can take passive voice marking.
The past and present participles are not specialized, but there is a past passive participle relativizing direct objects/patient only.
The past and present participles can relativize all core and some non-core participants.
The suffix -tāl ~ -tal is not transparent from a syncronous perspective.
However, the present participle is actually a non-past participle, referring both to the present and the future.

Source_Comment: Jeremy Bradley, Csilla Horváth, and Elena Skribnik (p.c. 2020)

Comment: Agentive nominalizations use lexical nominalizers like xum 'person' with a participle.
Source_Comment: Jeremy Bradley, Csilla Horváth, and Elena Skribnik (p.c. 2020)

Comment: Same word order as in declarative clauses - OV - unless it appears in emphatic construction, esp. in newer Russian-influenced texts (due to the focus position in Russian being in the sentence-final position).
As there is no genitive in Mansi, the nominative is used (i.e. zero marking) for both possessors and subjects of non-finite forms; but personal marking is with possessive suffixes.
The nominative is the only option for nouns; for pronouns there are oblique forms (traditionally called accusative, though the classification is problematic).
There are, however, also adpositions which do not make such a distinction.

Oblique forms (traditionally called accusative, though the classification is problematic), e.g., ānəm 'me', tawe 'him/her'; originally they are pronouns with possessive markers of the same person; they can take other case markers, e.g., ānəm-nəl (1SG.OBL-ABL) 'from me'.
The nominative case is used.
The numeral kit 'two' can be followed by singular or dual. Plural is not used with numerals.
See UT086.

However, there is a suffix -tal ~ -tāl traditionally classified as a privative derivational suffix (when used denominally) and the negative participle when used deverbally. But! Denominal forms can also be used as indirect objects, e.g. neː-taːl χos oːl-əs (woman-PRIV long live-PST.3SG) '(he) lived without a wife for a long time' (OUDB > northern mansi > 1239: 4). Thus one could in theory call this a case suffix too.
The dative (traditionally called lative) has two basic functions: 1) indirect object, 2) goal of a movement.
Additionally there is a comitative postposition used both with animate nouns and personal pronouns.
A postpositional construction is used.
The respective case (called the translative) has both functions.
Cf. sam-pal 'one eye' (lit. half an eye). With possessive marking the dual appears: sam-aγ-e 'his (two) eyes'.
The possessor is always unmarked.
Possessive suffixes mark number of the possessee and for the number and person of the possessor; in some suffix variants these markers are fused.
Whether the possessive suffix indicates possession or definiteness, becomes clear from the context.
The distinction is humans vs. non-humans, xoːŋxɑ/χotjut 'who' is used with people and manər 'what' with animals or inanimate objects.
There are no relative pronouns, only in very modern texts as obvious Russian influence.
There are no relative pronouns, only in very modern texts as obvious Russian influence.
The order is stem + possessive suffix + case suffix.

Source_Comment: Jeremy Bradley, Csilla Horváth, and Elena Skribnik (p.c. 2020)

Comment: No length opposition in the consonant system.
The primary stress is on the first syllable; vowels in non-first syllables are short.
No phonemic value.
Mansi has consistent first syllable stress.
Stress depends on the position and not on the weight of the syllable.
On phonological level there are no reduced vs. full contrastive subsets. In non-first syllables there is a middle, central [ǝ] but this is not a reduced vowel.
Only as a phonetic variant of /i/.
Present in Russian loanwords.
In three-constituent consonant clusters, the first constituent is the coda of the previous, the second and third consonants are the onset of the next syllable, cf. Coda-maximalization.
Only in monosyllabic words and in word-final syllables.

Three (or more) constituent clusters in stems are sporadic; they occur mostly in derivations or other affixed forms on morpheme boundaries. (In most cases a non-phonemic epenthetic schwa appears before the third or fourth constituent of non-homorganic clusters: njeːlǝm ‘tongue’ ⟶ njeːlm-ǝt tongue-PL, joxt-ǝs come-PST(.3SG) ⟶ joxt-sǝm come-PST.1SG.)
There is an opposition in palatalization between alveolars and alveopalatals (with the exception of the trill)
Present in Russian loanwords.
However, it does not occur in a word initial position.
There are no affricates in North Mansi.
There are no affricates in North Mansi.
Present in Russian loanwords.
Present in Russian loanwords.
There is contrast between /k/ vs. /kʷ/, /χ/ vs. /χʷ/.
However, it does not occur in a word initial position.
Present in Russian loanwords.
No gradations but paradigmatic alternations.
No gradations but paradigmatic alternations.
Modifiers precede their heads.
Modifiers precede their heads.
The collective suffix -ńś can be used with terms denoting kinship to express a collective or comitative meaning.
While nouns as direct objects are unmarked, personal pronouns have a special form (an oblique stem, traditionally called 'accusative').
The non-past tense (-i, -eː, -eːɣ) marker is used for both present and future.
The non-past tense (-i, -eː, -eːɣ) marker is used for both present and future.
Nouns as direct objects are unmarked (pronouns have a special form). Recipient can be encoded like the direct object or expressed with the dative case.
In the present tense, 3rd person clauses do not contain a copula, a copula may be occasionally used in other persons. However, copula is used in other tenses/moods.
No examples.
There is only a negative existential.
SV
Attested only sporadically; attributed to Russian influence.
The negation marker precedes the verb, but follows the preverb.
Superlatives may be expressed by reduplication.
There is an augmentative suffix kapaj.
Modifiers precede their heads.
The ablative case is used to mark the standard; it is also used to express source.
It is possible to use the comparative degree marker -nuw-; probably the result of Russian influence.
Not mentioned.
There is no complementizer.
There is no complementizer.
With nominal possessors the possessive suffix can be omitted.
E.g., the volitional particle wos(s) 'let'.

The only adjective that agrees with the noun in case (and number) is buorre 'good', but it does not fully agree in all cases. E.g., buorre ovdamearka (good.NOM example.NOM), buori ovdamearkka (good.GENACC example.GENACC), buriin ovdamearkkain (good.COM example.COM), but buori ovdamearkkas (good.GENACC example.LOC). Additionally, many if not most present-day speakers (and even language authorities) use the non-declined buorre (NOM) in all cases.
There are cases that do not show full agreement, but have genitive modifiers instead.
In non-finite clauses GENACC may be used.
There are many derivational suffixes with frequentative meaning.
The adverb aiddo 'just' makes the prxoimative function of the progressive (”action essive”) form even more explicit.
The so-called potential mood is somewhat different from its namesake in Finnish, but sometimes fits into this category.
Used colloquially, probably increasingly used due to Scandinavian influence.
There is also a purposive converb in -(a)ndihte ~ -(a)ndihtii.
Coordination can be expressed several ways:1) Ánná ja Piera 'Ánná and Piera', 2) Ánná(-guovttos) Piera-in (Ánná.GENACC(-DU) Piera-COM) 'Ánná with Piera'
The purposive infinitive (cf. UT043) can be negated with the element ama- followed by a possessive suffix.
The form čállin is traditionally the so-called agentive participle; nowadays it is often replaced by čállojuvvon (write.PASS.PST.PTCP) or čállán (write.PST.PTCP).
Somewhat surprisingly, the suffix -keahtes has been ignored by most grammars, but it can be labeled as the negative participle.

The agentive participle is homonymous with the action nominalization except for three-syllable stems, but even then, the agentive participle is identical with the short variant of the action nominalization used in compounds:čállin 1) write + agentive participle (’written by’) or 2) action nominalization ’(act of) writing’); jorgalan translate-AG.PTCP >< jorgaleapmi translate-AN, but jorgalan- in, e.g., jorgal-an-bargu translate-AN-work
The past participle is not commonly, but nevertheless occasionally used as an anterior converb.
Marginal, but possible.
The abessive case has degrammaticalized into postposition (and even adverb and preposition), but there are still traces of the abessive as a case marker.
There is a very much case-like suffix -ráigge that would in principle count as a case suffix to some extent, but is not a full case on a par with other cases.
In most spoken varieties possessive forms are very marginal and even quite foreign to many speakers.
gii ’who’ refers to people, mii ’what, which’ refers to animals, inanimate objects, and abstract concepts.
The dominant order is cx+px, but the relatively recent comitative plural is an exception.

Plural case forms are mostly distinguished from the singular ones by the use of a different set of case suffixes, even though historically these plural suffixes are bimorphemic and contain a plural marker *-j-. From a synchronic perspective, the question about the order of number and case suffixes is irrelevant, though the number and case markers cannot be distinguished.

The contrastive length of vowels is present in the Western Inland dialects and in the Eastern Inland dialects. They differ from each other a lot in terms of phonetic realization of single vowels and diphthongs. Length is not straight-forwardly marked in standard orthography.

Stress is assigned to the initial syllable of a foot, with primary stress on the initial foot, secondary stress on all other feet. This just happens to align with morphological boundaries in some cases, such as some derivational and even some inflectional suffixes. A purely phonological (i.e., prosodic) rule is completely sufficient to describe the stress pattern.
The first syllable of the initial foot always receives primary stress (all other inital syllable of non-initial feet receive secondary stress).

In South Saami, the dual is not obligatory in the language of first language speakers (cf. also Bergsland 1946:171). Usually, plural verb morphology is combined with dual personal pronouns: månnoeh vuejnebe (1DU see.1PL) 'we two see'. However, the dual is used in revitalized language/by second language speakers.

South Saami uses differential object marking (DOM) to express definiteness of objects in the plural. Plural objects marked with the nominative have an indefinite reading, plural objects marked with the accusative have a definite reading.

According to the literature, only the paradigm of the copula verb shows suppletion: the infinitive is arredh ‘to be’ (cf. the verb arredh ‘to be, live, dwell’), whereas most inflected forms are based on the stem lea-.
Many verbal suffixes commonly found in other Saamic languages (e.g., causatives, inchoatives, and reflexives) are either unattested or occur only rarely in South Saami (Kowalik 2023:374).
The copula can be omitted (Kowalik 2023:317).

There are no articles in South Saami, though the demonstrative pronoun dihte 'it, that' appears highly frequently in nominal phrases, where it resembles a definite article.
There are two different comparative constructions in South Saami: a locational comparative, which uses the elative case to mark the standard, and a particle-based comparative.
There are two different comparative constructions in South Saami: a locational comparative, which uses the elative case to mark the standard, and a particle-based comparative.

South Saami verbs are inflected for two moods, indicative and imperative. However, the three imperative forms (singular, dual, and plural) refer to second persons only and are often identical to the corresponding indicative present-tense forms.

A distinctive feature of Surgut Khanty consonantism is the presence of labialized velars (kw, ŋw, and most frequently ɣw) in environments where neighbouring Vakh dialects have plain velars preceded by rounded vowels, e.g. V jöŋ ‘ten’ = Surg jeŋw, V öɣi ‘girl’ = Surg ä̌ɣwi, V lök ‘road’ = Surg lekw, V joɣən ‘river’ = Surg jăɣwən. However, in ortography there is no distinction, ɣo, ko and ŋo are often written using w, k and ŋ.

Telic verbs in the present tense refer to the future. However, atelic verbs in the present tense can only refer to the future in the context of planned activities.
Only one source provides examples of special morphological marking of potential mood
Only one source provides examples of jussive forms
Copula can be used for predicate nominals in the 3rd person form of the present tense, but copula-drop is common.
Clauses with copula are common as well.
It cannot be excluded that participles on -m can relativise core participants alongside non-core participants, as its cognate sometimes does in Kildin Saami.
It cannot be excluded that participles on -m can relativise core participants alongside non-core participants, as its cognate sometimes does in Kildin Saami.
Szabó (1985) provides some examples of tentative possessive suffixes attached to participles (third person only), however, the interpretation of these examples remains somewhat doubtful.
Existing examples are ambiguous. They can be analysed as both ACC and GEN.
There is little evidence of such an agreement.
A relatively large group of nouns can form a separate instrumental case, but the commitative maintains polysemy.
Such examples are not attested in the corpus, but the possibility cannot be ruled out, since this phenomenon is common in the closely related Kildin Saami.
Number and case are marked comulatively within a case-number marker.

There is a strict animacy distinction for the concepts 'new' and 'young'. For the concept 'old', there is a syncretic term ᵛi̮ə̑m̜ɛs̜ 'old (animate/inanimate)' alongside a specialized term pĭ̮ə̑r̜ɛs̜ 'old (animate); elder' and a term used of reindeer mǡᵈt̜erii̭.
Quantitative contrast is accompanied by qualitative differences. Short vowels are centralised. Different phonological solutions are possible in this case.
When editing texts collected by Europaeus, T.I. Itkonen (1931) marks long ī in unstressed syllaables. Now it is reinterpreted as sequence ij (cf. Korhonen 1984).
T.I. Itkonen (1958) distinguished between glottal (voiceless vowel in his terms) and velar fricatives. However, Korhonen's (1984:323) description does not confirm this contrast.
[ɣ] is an allophone of /g/occurring before dental obstruents.
[w] is an allophone of /v/ occurring before other consonant and in word-final position.
Agent participle can be used as agentive nominalizations.
Participle on -m can be used as object or instumental nominalization.
Present tense is marked cumulatively with person and number.
Past tense is marked cumulatively with person and number.
Only the verb 'to be' allows the morphological marking of the future tense, other verbs use different periphrastic constructions.
There is a polysemy of reflexive and reciprocal meaning.
There is agreement only in the predicative position.
Constructions with floating qauntifier are common as well.
There are some examples in the corpus, but it is a marginal strategy.

In neighbouring closely related languages there is an inclusory construction. It is highly probable that this construction also existed in Ter Saami, but has not been attested due to the limited size of the corpus.
The existing corpus does not provide enough material on passive clauses.
Content interrogatives are possible in situ only in very rare cases.
The number of the object is indexed on the verb, e.g., 1SG meə-w° (singular object), 1SG meŋa-xəyu-n° (dual object), 1SG me-yə-n° (plural object); me- ‘to take, to do’.
However, number concord is more common.
In different-subject sentences, the lexical dependent subject is marked with the genitive.
The object of transitive verbs is marked with the accusative both in finite as well as non-finite clauses.
Objects are typically marked with the accusative case. However, in the imperative also the nominative case is possible.
There are the frequentatie-iterative-multiplicative suffixes -or, -ur, -er indicating that a situation is repeated, habitual or dispersive.
The future tense conveys both immediate as well as remote future.

Whereas imperfective verbs typically have progressive reading, the perfective verbs usually indicate remote past. In addition, a perfective verb may be used to denote an event, which occurred before another event in the past.
Aspectual morphology is expressed on the lexical verb.
The inferential evidential is also used in the mirative or exclamative function.
"In the 2nd person the meaning of the necessitative may be close to permissive (Nikolaeva 2014:91)".
The horative is used to express exhortations to the first person; it appears in 1SG, 1DU and 1PL.
The reflexive conjugation is formed based on the reflexive stem.
There is the purposive converb (final gerund).
The subject (god/sky/wheather) can be omitted although it is more common to express the subject.
The copula is not required in the past and present; the past is expressed on the adjective or noun, as in pidər° xanʹena-nə-sʹ° (you hunter-2SG-PST) 'you were a hunter' (Nikolaeva 2014:254).
Coordinating conjunctions can be found but this is not the commonest coordination strategy.
There are two existential copulas: tənʹa° (affirmative) and yəŋku (negative). Cf. standard negation: pida nʹī məncara-q (he NEG work-CONNEG) 'He did not work / he is not working'.
Modal, purposive, and conditional coverbs can be negated like finite verbs.
For instance, n'iwa negates action nominals.
Only transitive verbs are passivized.
When agreeing with the head noun, participles can take possessive suffixes.
See UT060.
There is no special agentive nominalization form, present participles are used regularly as agentive nominalizations.
The diminutive marking on verbs expresses the meaning 'for some time, for a while'.
There is no synthetic superlative. Superlatives are formed with the help of adverbs expressing 'very', 'most', e.g. s'am'anəxət° 'most'.
Pronominal object can be marked in the postposition.
Local postpositions usually appear in the locative (n´ana 'at'), dative (nʹah 'to'), (ablative nʹad° 'from') and prolative form (nʹamna 'along').

With numerals the quantified noun can stand in the singular or plural. In Tereshchenko (1965), it is usually in the singular. However, the speakers of the modern language prefer the plural, though may accept the singular as well.
The dative case also expresses lative meanings.
There is a caritive adverbial form that ends in -s'iq.
The dative case (-n°h ~ -t°h) is used; it is regarded as a local case.
There is the locative case, which additionally expresses instrumental and comitative meanings, means of transportation as well as cause.
The locative case may be used in both functions.
The derivational suffix -ngæ appears in a similar function, e.g. li 'bone', lingæ 'as a bone, for a bone'.
However, there is an adverbial form marked by the suffix -(də)r'em.
The predistinative is used.
Paired objects typically stand in plural, still, dual may be encountered as well - 'my eyes’ can be expressed either as sǣw°-xəyu-nʹi (DU) or as sǣw°-nʹi (PL).
Whereas the lexical possessor takes the genitive form, the pronominal possessor appears in the nominative form.
Pronominal possessor appears in the nominative form.
"[T]he 3rd person singular possessive affixes express identifiability based on deixis and situational context" (Nikolaeva 2014: 68). Their usage in this function is, however, optional.
/iː/, /uː/, /æː/
All three vowels are pronounced long or half-long and restricted to word-initial syllables.

Geminates are not generally typical of Tundra Nenets; if identical consonants become adjacent in the course of morphological derivation, degemination occurs, e.g., nʹum ‘name’ > nʹumʹi ‘my name (1SG)’ (<nʹum-mʹi).
Bisyllabic trochaic feet aligned to the left.
Stress is predictable from syllable and segmental structure. It falls on an initial syllable, syllables preceding a syllable with schwa, and non-final syllables precided by am unstressed syllable.
It represents a phonetic phenomenon.
It represents a phonetic phenomenon.
However, the vowel ǣ is pronounced as a raising diphthong in the Eastern dialects (Nikolaeva 2014:17).

The over-short vowel ə is typically pronounced as a very short a. The reduced vowel ° is either pronounced as a very short ə, i.e., mid central vowel, or it adds length to the preceding consonant (e.g., in word-final sequences C°q or C°h) or vowel.
There is only allophonic ɤ.
There is no opposition, since there is only long /æː/.
There are no consonantal clusters word-initially and word-finally; three-consonantal clusters are not allowed.
There are no consonantal clusters word-initially and word-finally; three-consonantal clusters are not allowed.
The maximal syllable structure is (C)V(C).
There are no consonantal clusters word-initially and word-finally; three-consonantal clusters are not allowed.
There are no consonantal clusters word-initially and word-finally; three-consonantal clusters are not allowed.
Most labial consonants have a palatalized (palato-labial) variant, in Forest Nenets there are palatalized velars as well.

b bʹ d dʹ c cʹ and k only occur in restricted positions, e.g., they do not stand word-initially or in a post-consonantal position, except that postconsonantal b appears as a result of the change of w in some stems
Like secondary consonants, r and rʹ do not occur word-initially, except in recent loanwords.
The following contrast can be observed: t'ukuº 'this', t'ikiº 'that (mostly anaphoric)', t'exa 'that, distant or behind something',takiº 'that (deictic)', tayºkuyº 'that one over there (visible)'.
The following demonstratives express such a distinction: t'exa 'that, distant or behind something' vs. tayºkuyº 'that one over there (visible)'.
Not mentioned explicitly but "[t]he only really irregular noun is ŋəmke 'what', which forms the accusative plural stem as ŋəwo" (Nikolaeva 2014:25).
The dual can carry associative meaning.
The productive suffixes are -s'eh and -bc'eh.
No gender.
No gender.
No gender.
No gender.
Inasmuch as possessive concord, which is generally optional, is only likely to occur in situations regarding inalienable relationships.
Like nouns, predicative adjectives express tense (inflectional past) and subject agreement.
Pronouns only have three grammatical cases.
Not mentioned.
Not mentioned.

A fairly large number of derivational suffixes are associated with perfective and imperfective aspect. For instance, the imperfectivizing suffix -(m)pe- ~-bə- expresses a durative meaning: tola- 'to count, to read' > toal-bə- 'to keep counting, to keep reading'.
There are several irregular stems which undergo vowel change, but the alternation seems to be motivated by tense and mood rather than person.
Not mentioned.
In the future tense and in oblique moods (e.g., probabilitive), the copula is required.
Conditional forms exist in which the verb 'to be' acts as an auxiliary, taking agreement marking and/or further mood marking.
The verb 'to be' can function as an auxialiary to provide tense information for nominal predicates.
Verbs referred to as 'light' are found in converb constructions.
Not mentioned.
Not mentioned.

Standard negation requires the periphrastic combination of a negative, TAM marked auxiliary n'i-, along with the main verb bearing the connegative suffix -q. As a strictly verb-final language, this suffix is in almost all cases clause final (including which non-verbal predicates, which require a clause-final copula also in the connegative mood).
The negative copula yəŋku is used to express existential negation.
Inferential and reportative moods can denote involuntary or uncontrolled events, but there is no dedicated marker or clear distinction in the sense of this feature.
Not mentioned, no examples - subject/object agreement is extensive enough to be sufficient.
Simultaneous and sequential clauses are overtly marked by participles/nominalizers, but all appear to carry (im)perfective aspect as well.
No gender.
No gender.
It also denotes an affectionate/positive attitude towards the referent.
It also denotes a negative/distasteful attitude towards the referent.
No gender.

The quantifier malºh 'all' is compatible with higher grammatical functions, namely, the subject, the direct object and sometimes the indirect object and the possessor. It is very marginal with other grammatical functions where the quantifying adjective xussuweyº 'each' is used.
Polar interrogatives are associated with particular intonation, namely, pitch raising on the penultimate and ultimate syllables, which may make the sentence final vowel longer.
The standard of comparison occurs in the ablative case.
The comparative affix is -rka.
The standard of comparison can be expressed by a construction with the postpositional word p'iruwºna 'compared to'.
The auxiliary 'to be' appears in the future tense and in non-indicative moods.
Formed from the dual or plural forms of the noun n'a 'companion, sibling, friend, relative'.
Pronominal possessors appear in the nominative, while lexical possessors take the genitive.
Not mentioned.
Not mentioned.
When the referent is new to the discourse, the dual is normally marked by the numeral quantifier s'id'a 'two' and a noun in the singular.
No gender.

There is a large number of moods, formed by suffixation – inferential, reportative, dubitative, probablitative, approximative, reputative and debitive - which all denote some form of inferential causation.
There are at least two interrogative verbs: xəqman- 'to say what' and its 'emphatic' variant xəqməl'e- ' to say what on earth'.

Non-verbal word order is free in simple clauses. In situ interrogatives appear to be as common as fronted forms, marked by raised intonation rather than dedicated syntactic positioning. Coded 1 as in situ is the default position within dependent clauses.
Not mentioned.
Not mentioned.
In 3Pl forms, the impersonal form is used.
Nowadays only the imperfect is used.
The negation is formed using a negative particle.
The analytic formation is, however, more common, e.g. Ana hiän tulou huomena (give.IMP.2G 3SG come.3SG tomorrow) 'Let him/her come tomorrow'.
Nowadays it is common to drop the copula, which can be regarded as Russian influence.
The accusative is only used with pronouns, e.g., miut 'me', miät 'us'.
Goal and location are marked the same way, e.g. naize-lla woman-ALL/ADE.
Goal and location are marked the same way, e.g. naize-lla woman-ALL/ADE.
External and internal local cases.
There are some indications that a third set started to develop but it never fully developed.
A postposition is used.
Still, kumb 'which' is more commonly used.
The order is fixed: case suffix + possessive suffix.
vanha/vahna 'old' can be used for inanimates as well as animates, however, there is a separate lexeme to denote old people - ruavaš.
ŋ is an allophone, e.g. henged [ˈheŋ.ged] 'souls'.
Only in loanwords, e.g. cuari [ˈt͡sua.ri] 'czar'.
However, further research is needed.
Only with words expressing close family relationships.
It is possible with attributively used parts of speech with a definite suffix.
Only in the past.
The present tense may be used for the expression of an action in the near future.
In the cases of attributively used parts of speech with a definite suffix.
However, VO is more common.
In modern language, due to Russian influence, plural forms are also possible.
There is syncretism in the possessive declension.
The dative can be used to express the result of change.
When the possessor is singular, there are two sets of PX for singular and plural possessee.
However, as a result of Russian influence the distinction animate vs. inanimate is emerging.
A separate relative pronoun кудӥз [kudiz] is used.
A separate relative pronoun кудӥз [kudiz] is used.
The plural marker precedes the case marker.
In imperative, negative prefixes.
Cf. Komi роч [rot͡ɕ] ‘Russian’.
Only in loanwords.
Only in new loanwords, e.g. вахта [vɑхtɑ] 'watch'.
Only in loanwords.
Can be marked with Px.
Can be marked with Px.
However, instrumental(-comitative) case "can be used as a coordination marker, coordinanting subjects (rarely direct objects or other arguments)" (Edygarova 2022:511).
The reflexive pronoun aśmeos is used as an inclusive 'we' ('I/we + hearer(s)'), while the personal pronoun mi ’we’ cannot be used with the inclusive meaning.
There is only proximal vs. distal distinction: ta 'this (here)' vs. distal so 'that (there)'.
There is only proximal vs. distal distinction: ta 'this (here)' vs. distal so 'that (there)'.
There is only proximal vs. distal distinction: ta 'this (here)' vs. distal so 'that (there)'.
Indirectly mentioned.
Indirectly mentioned; paired nouns like 'eyes', 'ears' usually appear in singular.
There is allomorphy in the plural marking: the suffix -jos- is attached after consonants and the suffix -os- after vowels.
Simple plural marker can take this function.

Besides the morphological distinction, e.g. йырыд /jɨr-ɨd/ 'your head', vynyd /vin-ɨd/ 'your younger brother' vs. коркаед /korka-jed/ 'your house', there are differences regarding the constructions: inalienable N:NOM + N, alienable N:ELA + N
Direct object: accusative case (following some semantic/grammatical conditions); indirect object: dative; agent: in some constructions marked with Instrumental, Dative, Genitive
Direct object: accusative case (following some semantic/grammatical conditions); indirect object: dative; agent: in some constructions marked with Instrumental, Dative, Genitive
Prepositions are used only marginally; all in all there are three prepositions: bi̮den, koti̮r 'per, one by one' and og 'about'.
Preterite I vs. Perfect (I), perfect expresses result while preterite does not entail such a meaning.
See also GB089.
Verb stems do not alter.
However, in constructions with verba dicendi and sentiendi the direct object can be marked with Dative, i.e. like the recipient.
Only lexicalized cases like ber-e kylyny (lit. 'stay in the back') + ACC or ABL 'to be late, miss' (Winkler 2011:127), uli karyny (lit. 'to do/make below') (ibid.:128)
Only for the verb 'be'.
The suffix -ti- turns the intransitive verb into transitive, e.g. tupany 'fix, fit' vs. tupatiny 'repair'.
The suffix -śki- turns a transitive verb into reflexive, e.g. dyśany 'put (something) on' vs. dyśaśkiny 'dress oneself'.
karyny 'do': with infinitives from Russian, some ideophones, routines and nouns.
See also GB126.
There is no principal difference in word order between an subordinated and a non-subordinate clause.
The verb can precede the clausal object.
The suffix -śky- is primarily reflexive but it can turn transitive auditory and visual perceptive verbs 'see' and 'hear' into passive 'be seen', 'be heard'.
The suffix -śky- is primarily reflexive but it can be used as an antipassive marker with some verbs.
The suffix -sa is used for simultaneity and precedence of the action, the suffix -ku for simultaneity only.
The suffix -ti- for causatives.
However, the complementizer šuysa deriving from the verb 'say' can be used in reason and purpose constructions (Klumpp 2016:540)
Total reduplication of a verb can express the longer duration of an imperfective non-pluractional event (Asztalos et al. 2019).

At least, inflected nouns can be reduplicated, expressing a higher quantity of a quality (augmentative): kužmyś-kužmyś baśtyny 'strength.ELA-strength.ELA take-INF' 'to take away with all his/her might.' It seems that mainly nouns with quantificational semantics and numeratives are productively reduplicated, e.g., kyrymen-kyrymen 'by bits', ćuren-ćuren 'in rows', etc.
Adjectives and adverbs reduplicated to express a superlative-like meaning.

However, see Napol'skich (2003:296), e.g., suffixes -ok (nyl 'girl' > nylok 'little/dear daughter'), -aš (pi 'boy, son' > pijaš 'little/dear boy'). According to Napol'skich (2003:296), these suffixes may also be "a kind of vocative suffixes."
They differ formally byde 'every', bydes 'all' and in the order possibilities: both UQ-N for 'all' and only UQ-N for 'every'
With the postposition dor-yn 'at-INE'
A possessor is in the genitive.
A polar interrogative particle typically occurs on the focus of the questioned constitent.
See GB148.
Only impersonal constructions allow overt Agent marking.
None of the past tenses seem to indicate the distance from the Time of Reference.
There is only singular vs. Plural distinction.

The perfect (participle) form vylem of the verb 'be' is used as the inferential marker. The perfect tense can also be used with evidential overtones, signaling that the speaker has not witnessed the event. The verbal form vylem can also form analytic past tenses, where it also carries evidential overtones (Winkler 2011:98).
This is attested in Beserman Udmurt.
This is attested in Beserman Udmurt.
Definite objects acquire accusative marking, while indefinite objects remain unmarked.
Indefinite objects remain in the nominative case like the subject of the clause.

The preposed Russian complementizer čto is replicated into Udmurt and can be used with verbs of thinking and/or knowing instead of the autochthonous complementizer šuysa (see Tanczos 2013 and Klumpp 2016)
Both the genitive and the ablative cases occur as markers of the possessor.
The optative is expressed by means of the particle med + FUT.
Analytical past tenses use the particalized verbal form val.
Analytical past tenses use the particalized verbal form val.
Non-finite forms are used mostly in construction, where they function as copula arguments.
Non-finite forms are used mostly in construction, where they function as copula arguments.

Some evidence suggests that the Ullisjaure dialect uses ACC to mark definite plural objects and NOM to mark indefinite objects. However, other dialects do not exhibit any traces of differential object marking.

There is no differential subject marking itself. However, marking the subject for number still depends on animacy and referential status to some extent. A pronominal subject can only be marked for dual if it is a definite human. Otherwise, the plural form is used for dual objects, animals, or indefinite humans.
There is no special marking of conditional mood in von Gerten's (2015), however, earlier documentations still provide some examples (see Schlachter 1958:XVIII; Larsson 2012:135-137).
There is no special marking of potential mood in von Gerten's (2015), however, earlier documentations still provide some examples (see Schlachter 1958:XVIII; Larsson 2012:135-137).
Predominantly plural, but singular is also possible.
Some dialects demonstrate /r/ in the place of /ð/, originating from Proto-Saami *δ.
No examples provided, possibly rare.
Examples of 'be' + supine inessive are possible but infrequent.
Not used in the standardised written language, sometimes can be heard in a spoken language.
Typically, the conditional mood is used.
There are only some rudiments, e.g., tä hiit kivi järve (3SG throw.3SG stone.GEN lake.ILL) 's/he will throw the stone into the lake' vs. tä hiitäs pikäle (3SG throw.REFL.3SG down) 's/he lies down'.

Only in imperfect forms, where one of the endings in both the second and the third person plural is a glottal stop. In both cases, it is neither the only nor the most frequently used ending, but it is nevertheless in use and can be used with all verbs. Pronoun is mostly dropped. In present tense forms, the glottal stop is not the ending for 2PL and 3PL.
It is possible for the main verb of a purpose clause to be in the infinitive form, but usually it is a finite verb in indicative or in conditional mood.
It is common to use a copula, e.g. ma olõ väläh (1SG be[1SG] outside) 'I am outside'. While playing a card game one could also say ma väläh (1SG out) 'I'm out’.
Passive past participle relativizes direct objects.
It is also possible to use joogu-i / ärq jooguq tsäi-d (drink.IMP.2SG-NEG / NEG.IMP.2SG drink.CNG tea-PRT) 'Don't drink tea!'.
The suffix may be attached only to the nominal form, e.g. käü-mise-kene (go-NMLZ-DIM) 'going'.
Forms like netilde 'via internet' are regarded as adverbs.
Other local relations are expressed by postpositions.
The inessive case is used, e.g., tä om Talna-h ministri-h (3SG be.3SG Tallinn-INE minister-INE) 'S/he is a minster in Tallinn'.
Although the relativizer usually makes difference between animate and inanimate referents, the border between the two may vary, thus, tools and other inanimates could be used with 'who'.
Only nuur 'young' and vahtsõnõ 'new' differ, not vana 'old'.
/ø/ is restricted to the first syllable only.
Often used instead of the present active participle, e.g. haukja pini 'barking dog', literally 'barker dog' (Iva 2007:109).

Preverbal negation can also be used. Postverbal negation particle ei is often assimilated with the verb, becoming a suffix, e.g., tulõ ei > tulõ-õi 'doesn’t come'. Negation in Võro South Estonian is unmarked for person.
However, it is also possible to use the standard negation particle ei 'no' in prohibitive constructions, e.g., andku eiq 'don't give!' (Iva 2007:102).
The negation particle can also occur postverbally, e.g., olõ ei, olõ-õi ’is not’.
Võro negation word ei has the distinction between the present (ei) and past (es) tense, but it has lost the person and number marking.
Võro negation word ei has lost the person and number marking, but it has the distinction between the present (ei) and past (es) tense.
The quotative is marked by -v and -vAt in both written and spoken Võro. Additionally, southwestern dialects employ the suffix -bEv, as in saabõv 'they say it is getting' (Iva 2007:96)
Participial relative clauses.
Not mentioned.
Such constructions are rare.
Potential mood has only survived in folksongs (Ariste 1968:74).
An alternative is to use the particle la + main verb in the indicative form.
The conditional mood is used.
The accusative case is used only with plural personal pronouns (Markus and Rozhanskiy_Votic 2022:337).
The abessive case has often been replaced by construction ilman 'without' + partitive. In recent recordings, abessive only occurs on the supine forms (see Markus and Rozhanskiy_Votic 2022:335).
There are some examples of prolatives, e.g., meitsii 'through us, via us', vezittse 'through water, by water'. These are usually regarded as adverbs.
Possible due to reduction (Ariste 1948:30-32). In recent Luuditsa Votic, the allative, and the adessive case have coincided (Markus and Rozhanskiy_Votic 2022:334-335).
However, kumpa 'which, who, what' is more common.
Possible, but not common, e.g., pit-i ettsi-ä einä, tšen praavitt-i (must-PST.3SG look_for-INF plant who cure-PST.3SG) 'One had to look for a plant that would cure'.
There are a few compound words which are no longer treated as compounds, e.g. parˈaiko 'now', sesˈama 'the very one' (Ariste 1968:15).
The last Votic speakers used vowel /ɨ/ in Russian loanwords (Markus and Rozhanskiy_Votic 2022:331; see also Ariste 1968:1).
The occurrence of word-initial consonant clusters is characteristic primarily of loans and onomatopoetic-descriptive words (Ariste 1968:11; see also Markus and Rozhanskiy_Votic 2022:332)
The velar nasal /ŋ/ occurs only as a combinatory phone before /g/ and /k/ (Ariste 1968:8).
Only in some descriptive words and loanwords, e.g., šarizõb [ˈʃa.ri.zɤp] sizzle.PRS.3SG
Only in borrowings (Ariste 1968:8).
Only in onomatopoetic-descriptive vocabulary, e.g., furskaa 'to snort', and in borrowings (Ariste 1968:9).
The numeral comes before the noun.
An adnominal demonstrative precedes the noun it modifies.
The possessor typically comes before the possessed noun.
S precedes V.
The negative auxiliary verb e-/õ- is used for declarative negation and the negator elä is used for negative imperative.
However, the role of intonation in polar interrogation needs further research.
There are several moods.
Modifiers typically precede the head noun.
Modifiers typically precede the head noun.
The locative case can be used used for the comitative.
The suffix -xazo / -kazo / -gazo is used to derive an agent noun from a verb.
The possesor precedes the possessed noun.
Personal pronouns inflect for the nominative, genitive, and accusative. However, genitive rarely occurs.
Each verb is lexically perfective or imperfective, there is no morphological distinction between them.
Direct objects appear in the nominative or oblique case, while recipients are typically coded with the dative or predestinative case.
There are various causative suffixes (marginal applicative uses).
There is a reflexive pronoun.
There is a reciprocal pronoun.
Pragmatically neutral word order in transitive clauses is SOV (see GB133). Finite complement clauses follow the structure SVmatrix COMPL (=OBJ).
Adnominal property words show no inflection. However, since the plural marker -ʔ is often realized as zero, it cannot be ruled out that occasionally they can agree in number.
Modifiers usually precede the head noun.
all' is a floating quantifier.
There is no special morphology.
Possible with past time reference.
Alternative questions can be formed using repetition of the clause.
The dative is used to mark an agent in a passive construction.
